title: 40 year anniversary special | Penn State’s stat leaders in the 1986 national championship season
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
In order to capture Penn State’s second national championship, Joe Paterno needed each starting player to contribute at a championship level.

The 1986 Nittany Lions were elite on both sides of the football field, with a third-ranked defense and offense ranking in the top-20. They were a complete roster that leaned on one another when a certain aspect of the game plan went awry, giving Penn State different options when it came down to preparing for each individual game.

Four decades after the win over Miami on Jan. 2, 1987 in the Fiesta Bowl, here are the stat category leaders from the Nittany Lions who won it all and their contributions in the biggest moments.

Passing: John Shaffer

John Shaffer experienced what he said was the “most anxiety-ridden, pressure-packed time” of his life during the training camps leading up to the 1986 season due to a quarterback position battle with backup Matt Knizner.

Shaffer’s junior season the year prior ended with him faltering in the Orange Bowl for the only loss of his career, leading to controversy on whether he’d start as a senior.

Shaffer won the job, completing 55.9% of his passes for 1,510 passing yards and nine touchdowns with four interceptions in his second season as the starting quarterback. He finished his career with a 66-1 record.

Although he led the Nittany Lions to yet another undefeated season leading up to the bowl game, his passing game once again faltered in the big moment, going 5-16 for 53 yards against Miami.

However, he had a big completion to wide receiver Eric Hamilton to convert on third-and-12 before later calling his own number to tie the game, 7-7, at halftime.

But Shaffer’s regular season actions were more than satisfactory in a noticeably weak schedule, as Penn State remained independent. Shaffer and company silenced the doubt for good and beat No. 2 Alabama, 23-3, on the road to improve to 7-0, with the passer going 13-17 for 168 yards in arguably the best game of his career.

Rushing: D.J. Dozier

When Shaffer struggled in the passing game, the running backs picked up the slack. And in a season where three Penn State running backs were drafted within the first three rounds of the 1987 NFL Draft, D.J. Dozier was the star of the show.

Down 10-7 in the fourth quarter of the Fiesta Bowl, Dozier took a handoff from Shaffer on the 6-yard line and made an inside cut through an open lane before effortlessly shedding one last defender for the championship-winning touchdown.

But Dozier was used to scoring big, game-clinching touchdowns on the ground. He had already sealed the deal against the Crimson Tide two months back and was ready for the moment to take home the title.

It capped off a solid season for the senior running back who was named a consensus All-American.

While it wasn’t his best year as a Nittany Lion on paper with that honor going to his 1,002 yards as a freshman, it was his best as a multi-skilled back as he dipped more into receiving in 1986.

Despite a refocusing on being a threat in many situations, Dozier finished his senior campaign with 811 rushing yards on 171 attempts and scored a career-high 10 touchdowns. He was later drafted 14th overall in the 1987 NFL Draft by the Minnesota Vikings.

Receiving: Eric Hamilton

The 1980s were a different time, with modern-day rules not yet put in place to allow the passing game to flourish as it does in the 21st century, such as calls like roughing the passer.

However, Eric Hamilton was the receiver with the most receiving yards for the Nittany Lions in the championship season. He racked up 387 yards and four touchdowns, averaging 20.4 yards per reception in a system that spread attempts evenly between him, Dozier, Ray Roundtree and tight end Brian Silverling.

Hamilton was targeted 19 times over the course of his senior season and made the most of it when his name was called. Hamilton averaged 35.2 yards per game in 1986 as a key contributor when the game plan called for a big gain.

Hamilton had an 82-yard receiving touchdown — thrown by Knizner — in Penn State’s 34-14 victory over Pitt in the final game of the regular season to secure a spot in the Fiesta Bowl. The pass was one of the longest passes made in Penn State’s history, currently at the No. 9 spot.

Tackles: Shane Conlan

Penn State’s reputation as “Linebacker U” dates back to the late 1960s, and Shane Conlan’s tenure as the leading defender didn’t disappoint.

Conlan led the Nittany Lions with 79 tackles on the season, 63 of which were solo efforts.

In the Fiesta Bowl, Conlan had eight tackles and a pair of interceptions, making his presence felt in a game that was largely a defensive standoff. The first of his interceptions came in the third quarter and deep in Penn State territory, as Miami quarterback Vinny Testaverde lost his footing in the pocket and threw a weak pass right to Conlan.

It’s the second interception that would go down in history as the play that won Penn State the national championship. While Dozier was the one to run the football into the end zone, Conlan’s pick was a 38-yard return to the 6-yard line, setting up Dozier’s inside rush for the go-ahead score.

He was later named an All-American for the second consecutive year and was a finalist for the Butkus Award given to the nation’s top linebacker. He was the highest drafted Penn State player in the 1987 NFL Draft, going No. 8 overall to the Buffalo Bills and eventually became a three-time All-Pro selection.

Interceptions: Duffy Cobbs

In the opening moments of the second quarter of the Fiesta Bowl, Testaverde had plenty of time to maneuver out of the pocket and send the ball upfield. However, Duffy Cobbs, who led Penn State in interceptions that season, positioned himself in the right place at the right time, easily picking off the pass on a potential scoring drive.

Cobbs had four interceptions on the season, including his grab in the Fiesta Bowl and another crucial interception against Syracuse in the Homecoming game on the first play of the matchup.

He went on to sign with the New England Patriots as an undrafted free agent following the 1987 NFL Draft, appearing in three games.

Kicking: Massimo Manca

Despite putting in a down year in terms of production compared to his first season as the full-time starter, Massimo Manca still set the record for most points scored by a kicker at the time in 1986 with 206 in his career. That record has since slid to the No. 14 spot.

In the 1986 season, Manca converted all 37 of his extra point attempts, but went 14-23 on field goals to cap off a relatively turbulent collegiate career. Three of his field goal completions came in the victory over Alabama, padding the lead for a convincing win.

In the Fiesta Bowl, Manca went 2-for-2 on extra points with a missed field goal from 50 yards out. His second extra point pushed the lead to 14-10 to ward off a potential chance at overtime, as Miami was left with no choice but to go for the touchdown on the last play of the game.

Manca went on to sign with the Cincinnati Bengals as an undrafted free agent and bounced around the league for the next two years on practice squads before finishing his career with the Barcelona Dragons of the World League of American Football, otherwise known as NFL Europe.

title: 2027 TE Sean Currie commits to Penn State
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has its second tight end commit in the Class of 2027.

Sean Currie announced his commitment to Penn State on Friday via the Rivals YouTube channel. He was formerly committed to Virginia for both football and lacrosse, but backed off his pledge in March.

Currie, a 6-foot-7, 230-pounder out of Lawrenceville, New Jersey, ranks as the No. 15 player in his home state and the No. 32 tight end in his class, per 247Sports composite.

The 3-star recruit chose the Nittany Lions over other elite Power Four programs such as Ohio State, Texas A&M, Vanderbilt and Georgia Tech.

In his junior year of high school at The Lawrenceville School, Currie had 34 receptions for 474 yards and eight touchdowns. He also played defensive back, recording three sacks and a pair of tackles for loss.

He has a date set for an official visit to Happy Valley on June 19. However, offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser, running backs coach Savon Huggins and quarterbacks coach Jake Waters all came out to visit him and his family for dinner on May 8.

He joins Cooper Terwilliger as the second tight end in the 2027 class.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State picks up commit from 2027 DL Aniti Paiva
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Ikaika Malloe has added another piece to his defensive line in the 2027 class.

Defensive lineman Aniti Paiva has committed to Penn State, he announced via Instagram on Friday.

The 6-foot-2, 315-pound lineman ranks as the No. 1017 player in the country, No. 112 defensive lineman and No. 12 player in Utah.

Paiva had offers from many schools on the West Coast, but Penn State was hot on his trail after offering him on Jan. 29. The Nittany Lions beat out Arizona, Boise State and Utah State for the defensive lineman.

It’s a new era at Penn State, as Paiva marks the first high school recruit from Utah since at least 1999.

Penn State will welcome Paiva for an official visit on June 12, one of just two official visits scheduled for the now-Nittany Lion pledge.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State LB Tony Rojas named to Lott IMPACT Trophy watch list
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's top linebacker has been recognized for his abilities on the field as well as his character off the field.

Tony Rojas was named to the Lott IMPACT Trophy watch list on Friday, given annually to the nation's best defensive player of the year who displays traits of its namesake, Pro Football Hall of Famer Ronnie Lott.

Rojas, if selected as winner, would follow in the footsteps of former Nittany Lion Carl Nassib, who won the award in 2015.

IMPACT stands for Integrity, Maturity, Performance, Academics, Community and Tenacity, and is the criteria to be selected for the award.

In 2024, Rojas was key to Penn State's defensive success throughout the season, making 58 tackles including six tackles for loss with three interceptions, earning himself an All-Big Ten honorable mention.

Rojas was injured during practice last season ahead of Penn State's matchup against UCLA, knocking him out for the rest of the year. He was on the watch list for the Hispanic College Football Player of the Year award.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Film review | 3-star RB Chukwuma Odoh brings speed, strength to Penn State
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
3-star Chukwuma Odoh became the second running back commit in a week, pledging to the Nittany Lions last Thursday and capping off a big week for the future of Penn State’s running back room.

Odoh, the No. 57-ranked back in his class, possesses both speed and toughness in his run game. Meanwhile, 4-star commit Aiden Gibson brings more downhill running and receiving.

While Odoh missed a pair of games during his junior season, he still made a big impact on the field with 929 yards and 13 touchdowns. That was a down year, however, when compared to his sophomore season during which he ran for 1,418 yards, including 10 100-yard games and four games that broke for over 150 yards.

Here’s a look at what the New Jersey native can bring to the Nittany Lions’ running back room.

Downfield speed

On the field and on the track, Odoh shows quick speed that’s capable of taking him to the end zone. Throughout his track career, he’s posted times of 10.86 seconds in the 100-meters and 22.34 seconds in the 200-meters, showcasing his raw speed.

During his junior year, Odoh rushed for nearly 10 yards per carry. Much of that success came from his speed, which allowed him to get off the line of scrimmage and down the field quickly.

As soon as Odoh has the ball in his hands, he bolts off the line and is quickly down the field.Here’s Odoh taking the hand off and immediately getting 15 yards downfield before he faces much defense: pic.twitter.com/Y9MOfOlr30— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

Not only does Odoh have the speed to be successful as a running back, but he’s doing it at 6-foot, 220 pounds. Odoh is comparable to Nick Singleton’s size and quickness on the field.

While Odoh is bursting out of the backfield, he pushes past several defenders as he fights for the first down. It took six defenders to take him down. pic.twitter.com/rSREMHrL1i— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

Despite being ranked 35 spots behind Gibson, Odoh’s speed could pair well with Gibson’s skillset to provide another backfield duo in the likes of Singleton and Kaytron Allen.

Big-gain playmaking

In addition to Odoh finding the end zone 13 times during his junior season, the young star had many more highlight-reel plays.

One of the biggest games of his career came against Immaculata in the NJSIAA Non-Public B quarterfinals, where he scored five touchdowns in his team’s 63-31 victory. A week later, he rushed for 301 yards and three touchdowns against Red Bank Catholic in the semifinals, showing he can perform on the biggest stages.

In the semifinals, Odoh utilized a juke move, stiff arm and spin move to move down the field before being pushed out of bounds pic.twitter.com/sIsp6bBwQ8— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

Odoh’s pass-catching skills were infrequently used during his first three years at Paramus Catholic. But when they were used, he continued to do what he does best and made more big plays.

He recorded five catches for 62 yards and a touchdown during his junior season, an increase from his sophomore campaign when he had just 55 yards and no score.

Odoh takes the short pass for another 35 yards after the catch, showcasing his ability to make big plays pic.twitter.com/gJ2ZiPB1fq— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

While Odoh doesn't have a lot of experience pass-catching, he's displayed he can perform by air or ground and could be a successful option through the air for the Nittany Lions.

High IQ play

Not only does Odoh have the size, speed and determination to get into the end zone, but he also has the IQ to make that scoring possible.

When he’s not rushing full steam ahead, Odoh plays with a Le’Veon Bell-style rushing attack, pausing at the line of scrimmage before making his move down the field.

After evaluating his options downfield, Odoh takes the ball the entire length of the field sideways to earn the first down, running much more than the roughly 10 yards on the stat sheet pic.twitter.com/ABsJTOlDmT— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

Even with size and speed playing to his advantage, Odoh doesn’t necessarily charge headfirst at defenders. Instead, he’s shown quick thinking as he finds the best gaps to make a play downfield.

In a crowded endzone, Odoh found his way through a tight gap between two blockers, adding another score to his stat sheet pic.twitter.com/pwuSx7mSLB— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

While he’s only a 3-star back and ranked as the No. 57 player at his position in the nation, Odoh has showcased his quickness, decision-making and toughness, giving him the potential to be a difference maker for the Nittany Lions.

title: Report: Penn State baseball's Nate Voss, Jack Porter enter transfer portal
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
After an underwhelming season, two of Penn State's regular starters in the coach Mike Gambino era have entered the transfer portal.

Catcher Nate Voss and outfielder Jack Porter have both reportedly entered the transfer portal, according to On3's new baseball transfer portal tracker.

The 2026 college baseball transfer portal tracker is live on @On3. Filter by position, school, conference and status — or search any player instantly. Bookmark it.Tracker: https://t.co/N3c1It3cDd pic.twitter.com/Ugt1FasIRA— Transfer Portal (@TransferPortal) May 20, 2026

Voss played in just one game during the 2026 season, suffering a season-ending injury. In 2025, the catcher hit .245, with 33 RBIs and a .496 on-base percentage.

Porter was a consistent two-year player for the Nittany Lions. Across his two years, he held a .310 batting average. In 2026, he notched 13 doubles and four home runs, both numbers lower than his 2025 mark.

title: Penn State men's lacrosse earns 4 USILA All-Americans
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
After a strong season, Penn State has earned some individual accolades.

Defenseman Alex Ross was named a First-Team All-American. Short-stick defenseman Jon King and attacker Hunter Aquino were both named to the third team, with Reid Gills earning honorable mention for his faceoff controls.

Award season continues…Alex Ross - 𝗙𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺Jon King - 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗿𝗱 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺Hunter Aquino - 𝗛𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗠𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻Reid Gills - 𝗛𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗠𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 pic.twitter.com/F8DBcKrE4x— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) May 21, 2026

Ross was the Nittany Lions' top defenseman, often locking down the opposing team's biggest threat on the offensive end. In addition to his on-ball defense, he totaled 35 ground balls, the most of his career.

King posed another threat on the defensive end, earning 38 ground balls. He turned defense into offense, scoring five goals and an assist across 16 games this year. Aquino, despite dealing with injuries all season long, scored 26 goals and nine assists, with a 39.4% shot percentage.

At the faceoff circle, Gills finished 10th in the country with a .612 winning percentage on faceoffs, going 123-78 on the season.

Gills and Aquino will return for their third season with the blue and white, while Ross and King will both leave the team after ending their senior campaigns.

title: SEE IT: TNT to air Penn State men's hockey's Gavin McKenna documentary
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
A historic year just became more historic for a Nittany Lion.

TNT revealed a trailer for "The McKenna Project," an hour-long documentary on Gavin McKenna and his year leading up to the NHL draft. It'll air following Game 1 of the Eastern Conference Finals between the Carolina Hurricanes and the Montreal Canadiens.

“Pressure’s a privilege…it means I’m doing something right.”“The 𝐌𝐜𝐊𝐄𝐍𝐍𝐀 Project” 📹Tonight on TNT 📺 following Game One of the Eastern Conference Finals!! 🏒 #WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/UPYTjYbC5E— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) May 21, 2026

With the lofty expectations entering his freshman year at Penn State, TNT took a more in-depth look at him on and off the ice during his freshman campaign, letting the hockey world get to know the potential next big thing in the NHL.

The Whitehorse, Yukon, native finished his first year with the Nittany Lions with 51 points, consistently garnering the opposition's full attention when he held the puck. McKenna remains the projected top prospect in the 2026 NHL Draft.

author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State has part of its nonconference schedule revealed.

Tennessee revealed its full schedule for the 2026 season, revealing that it'll travel to Happy Valley to take on Penn State as part of a weekend tournament.

2026 Schedule Drop!We’ll see you on Rocky Top this Fall🔗: https://t.co/X5XMVezTzS#GBO🍊 pic.twitter.com/BOmFa1iigD— Tennessee Volleyball (@Vol_VBall) May 19, 2026

title: Penn State men’s hockey to play Niagara as part of 2026-27 slate
author: Lexie Linderman | Football Editor
description:
Penn State has another nonconference opponent.

The Nittany Lions will face Niagara on Dec. 31 and Jan. 2, the Purple Eagles announced Wednesday.

The 2026-27 Schedule is Here!!!#Uncommon pic.twitter.com/agB5Ha7vta— Niagara University Men's Hockey (@NiagaraMHKY) May 20, 2026

The two-game series will be a home-and-home for the squads, with Penn State hosting on New Year’s Eve.

Niagara is the second known team from the AHA to be playing the Nittany Lions this upcoming season, joining Sacred Heart.

The Purple Eagles went 13-23-1 in 2025-26.

title: Penn State men’s lacrosse earns commitment from graduate transfer Owen Tasse
author: Liana Tiamzon | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has added a new defenseman to its roster.

Owen Tasse committed to the Nittany Lions on Thursday, he announced on Instagram.

Tasse is a veteran player from St. Bonaventure that was brought in as one of the Atlantic 10’s top takeaway defenders.

The Ottawa, Ontario native appeared in 14 games for the Bonnies in 2026, causing 30 turnovers and scooping 59 ground balls. Those marks ranked third and seventh in the conference per game, respectively.

The graduate transfer also contributed offensively, notching three goals and an assist in his collegiate career.

Before St. Bonaventure, Tasse built an impressive résumé in Canada, helping his teams win multiple city championships and national box lacrosse titles, including the 2023 Minto Cup with the Burlington Blaze and the 2022 Founders Cup with the Nepean Knights. His father, Jason Tasse, also played professionally in the National Lacrosse League.

Tasse now heads to Happy Valley to add some new physicality and depth to Penn State’s defensive unit entering the 2027 season. He'll have a chance to compete for minutes, with longtime starter Alex Ross off to the PLL.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Indoor pickleball franchise set to open in State College
author: Taylor Mcgillvray | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Picklr, a rapidly expanding indoor pickleball franchise, announced plans to open its third facility in Pennsylvania in State College this summer.

The 27,000-square-foot club will be located in the Benner Pike Shops at 503 Benner Pike, in the former Big Lots building across from the new Happy Valley Casino.

The facility will feature nine indoor courts, a pro shop and a private event space. The courts will use the same surfacing as those used by The Professional Pickleball Association.

Former Penn State football player Steve Smith, class of 1999, will own and operate the State College location. Smith said returning to the area and opening the facility is especially meaningful because of his connection to Penn State.

Members will have access to open play, leagues, tournaments and training programs through The Picklr Academy, which offers instruction for players of all skill levels. Memberships will also include access to Picklr locations nationwide.

The State College location is part of the company’s continued national expansion, with more than 500 clubs and 5,000 courts currently in development across North America.

Prospective players can purchase memberships or book individual courts once the facility opens.

MORE NEWS COVERAGE

title: Recapping the careers of Penn State women’s lacrosse’s 6 seniors
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After four years of repping the blue and white, several Penn Staters took the field for the final time two weeks ago, capping off their collegiate careers.

From being named to the Tewaaraton Award watch list to earning First Team All-Big Ten, here’s a look at the legacies the six Nittany Lions left on their team.

While defender Maggie Driver is a senior, she announced her return for a fifth year on May 13 and won’t be discussed in detail.

Lauren Tinson

After taking on a bigger role with the team last season, defender Lauren Tinson stepped up to the plate her senior year. Tinson was named a team captain and started all 19 games for the first time in her career.

The Downingtown, Pennsylvania, native was among the best defenders on the team, sitting third with 23 caused turnovers in Year 4. In eight games this year, she recorded multiple turnovers, proving to be a crucial part of the defensive stand.

With 27 career starts through four seasons, Tinson wrapped up her collegiate career with a total of 31 ground balls and 28 caused turnovers.

Erika Ho

Erika Ho was a go-to attacker throughout her time at Penn State, from scoring one goal her freshman year to a career-high 46 goals her junior year, earning All-Big Ten First Team and IWLCA West/Midwest All-Region First Team honors.

After being named captain for her senior year and selected to the Tewaaraton Award watch list, Ho sustained an injury that sidelined her for a pair of games and off the starting lineup for another six games. However, she still finished Year 4 with the fourth-most goals on the team, tallying 35 goals and 14 assists.

Not only did the Frankfort, Illinois, native succeed on offense, but she was also dominant at the draw. During her junior year, she picked up 112 draw controls, adding another 17 her senior year.

Payton Wainman

While attacker Payton Wainman found success early in her career with 12 starts her sophomore year and seven as a junior, she took her performance to another level in her final campaign.

The Delray Beach, Florida, native finished Year 4 with 43 goals, good for third-best on the team. In addition to her high-scoring output, Wainman proved to be an efficient shooter, recording the second-best shot-on-goal percentage for players who scored 10-plus goals.

Wainman recorded six hat tricks during her final season in Happy Valley, including a career-high five goals against Drexel.

Emma Kelly

After sitting behind Ho on the draw control depth chart last season, attacker Emma Kelly took over at midfield this year, recording 120 draws.

While she was never the leading goal scorer on the team, Kelly still finished her collegiate career with 67 goals. The Bethpage, New York, native also found success causing turnovers from the offensive end, ending this season with the fifth-most caused turnovers on the team and her career with 23 turnovers after four years.

Maddie Greco

Midfielder Maddie Greco didn’t see the field much prior to her senior season, playing in just 22 games through three years. However, she started in all 19 games in Year 4 and made a big impact on the team.

The 5-foot, speedy midfielder tallied 10 goals and five assists, along with 11 ground balls, five caused turnovers and 11 draw controls her senior year — all career highs. Of her 10 goals, three came against Bucknell, where she recorded her only career hat trick.

Emily Bette

Goalkeeper Emily Bette has only played in three games during her four years at Penn State, recording just 16 minutes and 49 seconds of playing time.

Despite her limited time on the field, Bette wrapped up her career with a .500 save percentage, including three saves her senior year.

title: AHA announces 2026-27 conference schedule for Penn State women's hockey
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
After a program-best season, Penn State's conference schedule has been revealed.

The Atlantic Hockey America revealed the full conference slate for all seven teams for the upcoming season.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗹𝘂𝗲𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗻𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟲-𝟮𝟳 𝗶𝘀 𝗼𝘂𝘁. 🗺️🔒 Check out the AHA Women's conference slate as the race for the championship begins! 🏆🏒🔗: https://t.co/7TJq00njIM#NCAAHockey pic.twitter.com/HYUivJ5q4z— Atlantic Hockey America (@Atlantic_Hockey) May 20, 2026

Penn State will open the conference slate on Oct. 2-4, hosting Robert Morris before hitting the road for a two-game series against Syracuse the next week.

The Nittany Lions will return to Pegula Ice Arena the following week for a series against Lindenwood, before a week off from conference games. They'll travel back to New York to take on RIT to end October and begin November.

On Nov. 13 and 14, the blue and white will travel to the AHA runner-up Mercyhurst for an in-state clash in Erie before hosting Delaware for a two-game slate prior to the Thanksgiving break. Penn State will play one conference series in December, hosting Syracuse on Dec. 4 and 5 before a likely long break in the overall schedule.

To begin the new year, the Nittany Lions will travel to Robert Morris on Jan. 15 and 16, before hosting Mercyhurst on Jan. 22 and 23. The blue and white will be on the road in two of the final three weeks, traveling to Delaware before hosting RIT on Feb. 5 and 6.

Penn State closes out the regular season with a road series against Lindenwood on Feb. 12 and 13 before the start of the AHA Tournament.

Despite the conference games being revealed, the Nittany Lions' nonconference schedule is yet to be finalized, leaving open spots to build another strong nonconference resume.

title: Former Penn State pledge Gabe Jenkins commits to Colorado
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Another former Penn State commit has found his new home.

4-star defensive back Gabe Jenkins has officially committed to Colorado following his visit to Boulder.

Jenkins ranks as the No. 321 player in the country, No. 33 safety and No. 12 player in Pennsylvania.

Jenkins initially committed to Penn State on Aug. 18, before decommitting on Oct. 12, the same day as the firing of James Franklin.

Matt Campbell made a push for him right away, re-offering the Pittsburgh native on Dec. 10, even getting him to Happy Valley for Junior Day on Jan. 24.

Despite making that push, the defensive back chose Colorado just days after his official visit to the Buffaloes. He has an official visit scheduled with the Nittany Lions on June 5, with Campbell still pushing to add in-state talent in the 2027 class.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball names Colin McMillan as next head coach
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has its next head coach following Mark Pavlik's retirement.

The Nittany Lions promoted associate head coach Colin McMillan to the head coaching role, the team announced Wednesday.

Colin’s 20th year in Happy Valley will be as head coach!He is the fourth head coach in the 50-year history of the program. #WeAre pic.twitter.com/SWGMkS4dtz— Penn State Men's Volleyball (@PennStateMVBALL) May 20, 2026

"I am honored and humbled to be given the opportunity to be the next head coach of Penn State men's volleyball,” McMillan said in a news release. “To continue the legacy established by coach (Tom) Tait, coach (Tom) Peterson and coach Pavlik is a huge responsibility that I look forward to taking on. I would like to thank Dr. (Pat) Kraft and the administration for believing in our staff to lead this program and build upon the great foundation laid by all those that have come before us."

McMillan has been on Penn State's staff for the past 19 seasons, and the last 10 years have been as associate head coach. With McMillan on staff, the Nittany Lions won a national title in 2008, 14 EIVA Tournament championships and nine runs to the Final Four.

The middle blocker position has been McMillan's expertise on staff. He's helped coach Olympian Max Holt, who won a bronze medal with Team USA in the 2024 Olympics, and most recently Owen Rose, who is a three-time All-American.

“We are thrilled to name Colin as the next head coach of the men’s volleyball program at Penn State,” Kraft said in a news release. “He has played an integral role in the team’s success at the conference and national level in his nearly two decades working with coach Pavlik. His dedication to the program and his commitment to developing student-athletes at the collegiate level made him an easy choice to ensure Penn State continues to compete as a championship-caliber team.”

McMillan was a middle blocker during his playing days at Ohio State in the early 2000s, where he was a four-time All-MIVA selection and made the 2000 NC Championship All-Tournament team. He then started his coaching career as a graduate assistant for the Buckeyes in 2003.

Now, McMillan will become the fourth head coach in the history of Penn State's program.

"I am so grateful and so fortunate to have worked alongside Colin for the last 19 years,” Pavlik said in a news release. “He has been an outstanding role model for our guys as a coach, father and husband. His integrity, compassion and leadership have served our program so well. He has helped to mold many minds and lives - mine included. Now, as he becomes the next head coach of Penn State men's volleyball, I am thrilled to know our program will continue to improve. He knows and understands better than anyone the culture of this program, our student-athletes and the traditions which make this team special to all who came before. The future of Penn State men's volleyball is in great hands!"

title: Dueling column | Who should Penn State play in the 2026 White Out?
author: Noah Aberegg & Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 2026 season boasts a lot of changes for Penn State — a new head coach, a new quarterback and an almost completed set of stadium renovations.

But one thing this season will look the same as always. The Nittany Lions have held the White Out in some capacity for the past 22 years, drawing in some of the biggest crowds of all time to Happy Valley for some of the most anticipated matchups of the year.

Here are two games that Penn State could circle on the calendar for its annual White Out.

USC (Noah Aberegg)

Of the five home Big Ten games Penn State has scheduled this season, USC had the best record last year, and it’s also the only home opponent who finished the season ranked in the AP poll.

In a battle of two teams from opposite ends of the country, there’s been only nine matchups between the two sides, but the two most recent games have been instant classics — the Trojans’ comeback in the 2017 Rose Bowl and the Nittany Lions overtime win in 2024.

Considering that recent history, having USC as the White Out opponent is an easy choice.

While the game won’t be a top-10 matchup like last year’s record-breaking White Out between Oregon and Penn State, it could feature two teams who were ranked in ESPN’s latest top 25, which was released in mid-April.

Not only would it be a good matchup on the field, but having the White Out against USC could also highlight a new connection between the two teams in defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn.

After spending two years with the Trojans, Lynn was signed as Penn State’s next defensive coordinator in December. Not only did he experience the Nittany Lions’ nail-biting victory in 2024, but he played for Penn State from 2008-11, playing against the Trojans in 2009.

A White Out game against his previous team would be a special homecoming for Lynn by coaching his alma mater against the program he previously coached for.

Minnesota (Andrew Mercer)

When it comes to Penn State’s relationship with Minnesota, there’s an often overlooked historical piece involved that would make this season’s White Out all the more meaningful — the Governor’s Victory Bell trophy.

Therefore, contrary to popular opinion, I believe that Penn State should host Minnesota for the annual White Out game, especially considering the close finish between the two in 2024.

The pair first met in 1993, which was the Nittany Lions’ first-ever Big Ten conference game in program history. With Campbell coming to Happy Valley as the new head coach, playing the same opponent that Penn State played in its Big Ten debut as his first White Out would be poetic.

Additionally, Minnesota would be an easier team for Campbell’s White Out debut compared to USC. The Golden Gophers went 5-4 in the Big Ten last season, and Minnesota’s over/under on FanDuel is set to 6.5 wins this regular season, with -158 odds of the Golden Gophers winning less than that figure.

There’s not only the feat of winning Penn State’s signature home game on the line, but the fact that it’s Campbell’s first means he’ll likely wish to make the best impression on the fanbase.

The best way of doing so would be emerging victorious, something Penn State has done in a White Out against Minnesota previously. The Nittany Lions hosted the Golden Gophers in the annual spectacle in 2022, emerging with a 45-17 victory.

On the spectator side of things, there’s the possibility that broadcast rights to the USC game would be picked up by Fox’s Big Noon Kickoff. Nearly all prior White Out games were played at night, with the special exception of when Penn State faced SMU in the College Football Playoffs at noon.

With that said, should Fox claim the USC game and the time be set for noon, that would put Minnesota in prime position to be Penn State’s White Out opponent.

The only wrinkle with Minnesota as the White Out opponent is the date of the matchup. Because it's currently scheduled for Nov. 14, that means the Golden Gophers would have to agree to play at night, something they might choose not to do to avoid the White Out crowd.

Regardless, playing Minnesota in the White Out would be an easy way for Campbell to have a successful first experience coaching in that atmosphere.

title: Results are in for 2026 PA primary elections | Here's who will be on the Centre County ballot in November
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Centre County voters cast their ballots in the 2026 Pennsylvania primary elections, with polls closing at 8:00 p.m. on Tuesday.

A total of 20,315 ballots were cast for the 2026 Gubernatorial Primary, representing 24.37% of the county’s 83,360 registered voters, according to centrecountypa.gov.

Below are unofficial results of the 2026 Pennsylvania primary election as of 11:16 p.m. on Tuesday.

Democratic Governor

Incumbent Josh Shapiro ran uncontested for the Democratic ticket, receiving 11,443 total votes with 135 write-ins. Shapiro, the 48th governor of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, was first sworn into the Office of the Governor in 2023.

Republican Governor

Stacy Garrity, the Republican candidate for the gubernatorial nomination, ran uncontested and received 7,205 total votes, with 752 write-ins.

Garrity, a retired U.S. Army Reserve Colonel, served as the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania’s 78th treasurer.

Democratic Lieutenant Governor

Incumbent Austin Davis, who also ran uncontested for the Democratic nomination, received 11,314 total votes with 64 write-ins. Davis, who is the youngest Lieutenant Governor in the county, is also the first Black Lieutenant Governor.

The lieutenant governor serves as chair of the state Board of Pardons alongside the attorney general and three governor-appointed additional members, reviewing criminal cases to decide if a pardon or commutation should be suggested to the governor for final approval or denial, according to the state’s constitution.

Additionally, Pennsylvania’s Lieutenant Governor serves as President of the State Senate.

Republican Lieutenant Governor

Jason Richey ran against John Ventre for the Republican nomination and received 5,190 votes in total, pulling ahead of Ventre by 2,723 votes. Richey, who has worked as a litigator for over thirty years, was endorsed by Republican gubernatorial candidate Garrity.

Ventre, who did not win the election, received 2,467 votes. He is a member of the Committee of Seventy, which is a nonpartisan civil leadership initiative focused on advancing representative government through civilian engagement and advocacy, according to the organization’s website.

Democratic Representative in Congress

Ray Bilger ran uncontested for the Democratic nomination and received 11,412 total votes with 57 write-ins. Bilger, who has led teams for the Air Force, National Security Agency and State Department, was the NSA Technical Director for Cyber Analysis from 2015 to 2017.

Republican Representative in Congress

Incumbent Glenn “GT” Thompson ran uncontested for the Republican nomination and received 7,759 total votes with 258 write-ins. Thompson, who is a Penn State alum, currently represents the Fifteenth District in the House of Representatives.

Democratic Representative in the General Assembly 77th Legislative District

Incumbent Scott Conklin ran uncontested for the Democratic nomination and received 4,655 total votes with 34 write-ins. Conklin, who has been a member of the House of Representatives since 2006, was a primary sponsor of a bill that aimed to regulate the sale of land belonging to former state correctional institutions.

Republican Representative in the General Assembly 77th Legislative District

Michelle Schellberg also ran uncontested for the Republic nomination, receiving 2,501 total votes with 60 write-ins. Schellberg currently serves as chairwoman for the Center County Republican committee.

Democratic Representative in the General Assembly 82nd Legislative District

Incumbent Paul Takac ran uncontested and received 4,497 total votes with 13 write-ins for the Democratic nomination. Takac has represented the 82nd legislation district—which includes Penn State’s University Park campus—since 2022. On Oct. 1, 2025, he spoke at a Harrisburg press conference given by Conklin and Rep. Dallas Kephart, cosponsors of the aforementioned bill aiming to regulate the sale of former SCI land.

Republican Representative in the General Assembly 82nd Legislative District

Kirsten McTernan ran uncontested for the Republican nomination and received 2,644 total votes with 89 write-ins. She ran for the State College Area School Board in 2025.

Democratic Representative in the General Assembly 171st Legislative District

John Zangari, who ran unopposed for the Democratic nomination, received 2,220 total votes with 18 write-ins. In the past, Zangari served as a Lehigh County corrections officer, and he currently works as the Administrative and Facilities Assistant in Penn State’s Department of Sociology and Criminology at University Park.

Republican Representative in the General Assembly 171st Legislative District

Incumbent Kerry Benninghoff ran uncontested for the Republican nomination and received 2,524 total votes with 38 write-ins. Benninghoff, who currently represents the 171st Legislative District, also serves as Republican chairman of the House Transportation Committee. He is also a member of the Gaming Oversight and Rules committees.

The full list of Centre County unofficial General Primary Elections results is available at centrecountypa.gov. The next step for these candidates is the November 3 general elections.

title: Film review | 4-star RB Aiden Gibson possesses power running, receiving
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State earned a commitment from 4-star running back Aiden Gibson last week, who became the first of two running backs to pledge during the week. Gibson was joined by 3-star Chukwuma Odoh a few days later, giving the Nittany Lions a strong 2027 running back duo.

Gibson committed to Penn State as the No. 322 player in the class, No. 22 running back and No. 8 player in South Carolina. He’s added onto a recruiting class that has sought out talent from all over the country, with Gibson becoming the first player out of South Carolina in the class.

Gibson was a major get for the Nittany Lions, possessing strong talent from the running back position. Mixed with Odoh’s quickness comes Gibson’s thunder, potentially bringing that thunder and lightning duo back that Penn State had with Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen.

Here’s a deeper look at some things that stand out in Gibson's film.

Downhill running

While Gibson doesn’t have the home run speed that a runner like Odoh has, he still hits the hole hard and has a downhill running style.

Gibson showcased an ability to break off big runs with his capability to make quick decisions, helping find open gaps and get downhill quickly.

Gibson has a downhill running style, similar to that of former Nittany Lion Kaytron Allen, finding gaps quicklyHere’s him finding the gap and turning an outside run into a big gain: pic.twitter.com/RdZDWZn5aG— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

Penn State has had success with this style of runner, as Allen had a record-breaking senior year and has the most career rushing yards in program history. While Gibson likely won’t see the field as much as Allen did in his first season, the running styles are pretty similar.

Gibson doesn’t have home run speed, but he’s fast enough to have big runs and get in the end zone from far out pic.twitter.com/DY0Nkh6Hoe— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

Gibson’s downhill running allowed him to rush for over seven yards per carry in 2025, eclipsing 1,500 yards for a second consecutive season.

Tough to tackle

In addition to Gibson’s downhill running style comes his ability to break tackles. Across his tape, he often fights off tackles to extend run plays even longer.

Gibson has a strong ability to make defenders miss, using his balance and strength to fend off tackles pic.twitter.com/FYgFagAeUO— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

Gibson’s 6-foot-1, 212-pound frame could prove to be more college ready, allowing him to progress quicker in the offseason. His frame has made it easier to fend off tackles at the high school level, and with his powerful running he could see early playing time.

Not only is he strong and has firm balance, he has an underrated ability to make defenders miss with juke moves. The combination allows him to be a dynamic running back who can beat defenses in many different ways.

It’s not just his strength and balance that allows him to break tackles. He also has quick feet allowing him to juke past quicker cornerbacks pic.twitter.com/YRvhs1RJss— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

The South Carolina native’s power is next level, as he can run through tackles in addition to fending off defenders. This combo of power and strength with his downhill running makes for a very intriguing prospect and a name that could add to the recent success of Penn State running backs.

Receiving threat

It’s not just in the run game that Gibson makes an impact — he’s shown signs of being a reliable receiver in the pass game, whether it’s out of the backfield or lined up out wide.

Gibson shows a strong ability to catch the ball out of the backfield. Whether it’s screens or short routes, his hands allow him to quickly asses upfield to turn small plays into bigger ones pic.twitter.com/rbk0DsNPfv— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

In what will be a much more versatile offense at Penn State, Gibson’s ability to catch the ball will be vital for his future in the program. Gibson showed a plethora of routes, with catches in the backfield and an ability to win one-on-one down the field.

Last season, Gibson had 387 receiving yards and four receiving touchdowns, proving to be a versatile threat. While the offense he’ll be in at Penn State didn’t utilize the running backs in the pass game too much, it also didn’t have the receiving threat that a running back like Gibson provides.

His versatility showcases his ability to also be a threat out wide as an extra receiver pic.twitter.com/Kwb2lobm6A— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 18, 2026

Gibson proves he can be a difference maker time and time again, whether it’s getting handoffs or catching the ball. No matter how he’s utilized in the offense, he has the ability to make big plays and be a drive-changer at any given moment.

title: ‘He had an incredible game’ | Preston Hawkins posts career day despite tournament loss to No. 8 Princeton
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
NEWARK, Del. — As the sun beat down on the artificial turf at Delaware Stadium, on-field temperatures reached 112 degrees as No. 8 Penn State fell to No. 1 Princeton in the NCAA quarterfinal.

The FieldTurf surface emblazoned with a grinning Blue Hen wasn't the hottest thing in Newark on Sunday afternoon, however, as the dueling goaltenders stole the show in the Tigers’ 14-10 win.

“It was pretty brutal,” Princeton midfielder Jackson Green said. “These are big games, so you’ve just got to fight through it. The weather’s the same for both teams.”

Freshman netminder Preston Hawkins made his presence felt immediately, stuffing a shot from Colin Burns. The save sparked a transition opportunity for the blue and white, one that defenseman Alex Ross capitalized on with a tone-setting opening goal.

Hawkins notched six saves in the first quarter, and nine total in the first half, keeping the matchup knotted at 6-6 after a half hour of play.

“He had an incredible game,” Princeton midfielder Tucker Wade said. “It felt like throughout the game we were getting good looks, and just couldn’t get past him for a while.”

Eventually, Wade and the rest of the Tigers were able to find the nylon, scoring eight times in the second half. The junior out of Bethesda, Maryland, was most successful, scoring four times in the victory over the Nittany Lions.

Despite the loss, Hawkins made eight stops in the final two frames to set a new career-high of 17 saves, besting his former record of 14 that came in Penn State’s March 21 victory over Maryland in Happy Valley.

After the win against the Terrapins, the Powell, Ohio, native was ecstatic as he addressed the media. Two months later, Hawkins was in tears as he sat between teammate Chase Robertson and coach Jeff Tambroni inside the Bob Carpenter Center adjacent to the field where he’d just concluded his debut campaign as a Nittany Lion.

“Princeton’s a good team, they have a lot of good shooters,” Hawkins said. “The defense was in front of me, giving me the shots I wanted to see. They were the reason I was successful.”

While he buried his third career goal to begin the day, Ross anchored that unit in what was his final collegiate appearance. Hawkins credited the senior not only for his performance on Sunday, but for his leadership all season.

“I’ve looked up to him all year,” Hawkins said. “I wouldn’t be here without him. He’s given me so much guidance. He’s made me feel comfortable on the field.”

While he plays on the opposite end of the field, Robertson echoed his goaltender’s thoughts on the PLL-bound Ross.

“I’m just so thankful to have him as a teammate and as a friend,” Robertson said. “Every game he was always motivating us. He took me under his wing, and as a sophomore he helped me improve my game. He’s going to do great things in the future.”

80 yards away from Hawkins, Ryan Croddick delivered a juxtaposing but equally as important performance for Princeton. Croddick, the reigning Ivy League Goaltender of the Year, made eight saves in his squad’s win, but just two in the first half.

“Penn State has such an incredibly disciplined offense,” Princeton coach Matt Madalon said. “I don’t think they settled for a shot. With (Hunter) Aquino off the field, they’re not taking a shot outside of eight yards, so it’s not a game where a goalie is going to get a ton of rhythm.”

Six saves from the senior in the final two quarters saw the blue and white score just four goals, and were instrumental in ending the Nittany Lions’ season.

“We debated going into a zone to see if we could spark him with an outside save and get some transition,” Madalon said. “Luckily they settled, and he got into the game down the stretch, but (it was a) really hard game to play goalie in.”

While Croddick got his 14th victory on the season, Hawkins notched the sixth loss of his freshman year. The goaltender, alongside attackman Jack Iannantuono and midfielder Andrew Beard, played a key role in a trio of first-year stars that made an impact early in their time at Penn State.

In a season that had peaks and valleys, the brotherhood of the squad as a whole was something that the young stars could lean on, especially with a well-balanced group of seniors that have donned the blue and white for the last time.

“It hurt a lot to see my teammates cry, especially our seniors and our captains,” Robertson said. “I’m so thankful to have them as my brothers and best friends, and in my life (both) today and in the future.”

Despite a graduating class that left their impact on the program, Penn State is undoubtedly a young squad that will return the majority of their 2026 production. Tambroni, still searching for his first national championship, noted that his goal is to win while creating a lasting trend of high-level play.

“Every year, the mission is the same, to build and sustain a championship culture,” Tambroni said. “That’s where I’m most proud. I don’t think they failed today, I just think we lost today.”

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: ‘A wonderful group of young men’ | Senior class leaves loving culture despite loss in NCAA quarterfinals
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
NEWARK, Del. — Penn State’s senior class has played in four-consecutive NCAA tournaments. They’ve gotten knocked out in the first round, went to the national semifinals at Gillette Stadium and Lincoln Financial Field, but never have gotten over the hump with a title-game berth.

The 2026 season was supposed to be different. The Nittany Lions returned a senior class that featured one of the best defensemen in the nation, Alex Ross, as well as key contributors like Will Costin, Jon King and one of the deepest midfield cores in college lacrosse.

Despite falling 14-10 to No. 1 Princeton in the national quarterfinals on Sunday, the senior class left it all out there on the field and instilled a winning culture that the Nittany Lion youth will take forward in seasons to come.

“It hurts a lot to see my teammates, my brothers cry, especially our seniors and our captains,” sophomore Chase Robertson said. “They helped lead all the way down to our freshmen. I'm so thankful to have every single one of them, and it hurts to have our season go out this way.”

Robertson and freshman goaltender Preston Hawkins took to the podium following the loss with choked words. They noted that the senior class was instrumental to the team’s successes, on and off the field, fostering a welcoming environment that made the younger teammates feel at home.

“Despite the loss, there's just so much love in the locker room that you got 48 guys, coach included, just going up to each other, hugging each other, could tell each other anything,” Hawkins said. “This group's so tight, and everyone's there for everyone, so I mean, just the amount of love, it just never fades, regardless.”

Coach Jeff Tambroni, in his 26th season as a Division I head coach, has seen his fair share of senior leadership groups. Tambroni claimed that “this is one of the most balanced senior classes I've ever coached.”

“Now, statistically, it doesn't pop off the page,” Tambroni said. “You don't see a ton of goals, but you do in terms of the roles that they adopted, the confidence that they instilled, the sacrifices that they made.”

With their collegiate careers all said and done, the players will go off in different directions. Ross is headed to the New York Atlas of the Premier Lacrosse League, who Tambroni said will “likely have a long and successful PLL career.”

Others, however, may not continue at the professional level and will look back at their time at Panzer Stadium in Happy Valley. Tambroni’s way of thinking says that success isn’t built on the numbers or how many national championships are won, but if the seniors wish they did anything differently.

“My measure is when they stand up at the end of the year and they leave and we start talking about next year's team; can that group say ‘we left this program without any regrets?’” Tambroni said. “I hope that someday they'll all step back and say it was a phenomenal experience with a wonderful group of young men.”

title: Penn State track and field’s Allon Clay wins 2nd straight Big Ten outdoor title
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After taking home gold at last year’s Big Ten championship, graduate student Allon Clay has done it again.

Clay won the men’s 800-meter finals at the 2026 Outdoor Big Ten Track and Field Championships on Sunday, running 1:46.58.

Clay was in the lead after a 52.01-second first lap and continued to keep pace through the last 400-meters, running a 54.57 last lap to win the race.

The Fujisawa, Kanagawa, Japan, native had a stellar showing in the preliminary round on Saturday, running a meet record 1:45.11 to set the No. 3 mark in school history.

Clay is coming off an indoor season where he fell short in both the 800m and 600-meter finals. While finishing first and second in the prelims, he went on to finish second and fourth in the finals, respectively, before finishing third at the NCAA Championships.

Clay is the only Nittany Lion to have won the men’s 800m in nearly 10 years, having won the event the past two seasons.

title: Penn State track and field’s Hayley Kitching wins 4th Big Ten 800m title
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Like clockwork, Penn State’s Hayley Kitching has come out on top again.

Kitching won her fourth 800-meter Big Ten title during her collegiate career, running 2:01.77 at the 2026 Outdoor Big Ten Track and Field Championships.

Kitching came through the first lap in the lead, running a 59.29-second lap. Things stayed the same through the final lap, running 1:02.48 for the win.

The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native finished second in the 800m prelims on Saturday, running a 2:01.61. Kitching is coming off a Big Ten championship-winning indoor season, where she broke her former school record in 1:59.22.

The Nittany Lions have turned into an 800m powerhouse in recent years, as evident by Kitching’s four conference titles. Of the five women’s 800m Big Ten titles attributed to Penn State, Kitching is responsible for two of them.

Kitching entered the conference championship weekend with the 22nd-best mark in the East region and will look to carry her success to the NCAA East First Round later this month.

title: Film review | 3-star QB commit Will Wood brings strong arm, poise to Penn State
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State secured a quarterback commitment in the Class of 2027 when Will Wood — a 3-star from Westwood, Massachusetts — announced his pledge on Friday afternoon.

Wood ranks as the No. 3 player out of Massachusetts and No. 29 quarterback in his class, per 247Sports composite. He finished up his junior year at Xaverian Brothers High School with the program’s third-consecutive MIAA Division I State Championship after throwing for over 2,800 yards and 42 touchdowns with one interception.

So far in his two years as the starter, Wood has won a pair of state championships and earned a 21-6 overall record.

Wood’s tape offers much to look at, especially with his strong arm and ability to improvise when things get messy in the pocket. His arm, crafty play and mental strength will be crucial to his growth into his role as Penn State’s potential quarterback of the future.

Here’s a more in-depth look at some of his film.

Stellar deep ball, accuracy

One of Wood’s deadliest weapons is his deep ball. It’s accurate and hits receivers in stride, specifically primary wideout Christian McIntyre, who Wood has built trust with during his time with the Hawks.

From deeper in his own end, Wood can air out passes up the middle on dig or post routes for big gains between the hashmarks. Additionally, he does well with corner routes to give his receivers space up the sidelines away from the majority of defenders.

One of Wood’s best aspects is his arm. He’s got an accurate deep ball and hits his receivers in stride on post and corner routes for big gains in the midfield and up the sidelines.Here’s a few clips of Wood showcasing his throwing power and placement accuracy: pic.twitter.com/3NiMM6wNlP— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

In the red zone, Wood often elected to attack the back of the end zone with higher passes that his receivers had to leap for.

In the endzone, Wood continues to aim long, often attacking the back of the endzone with high passes.He’s put a lot of points on the board by using every inch of the field. pic.twitter.com/OSp6VX0HGG— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

Additionally, Wood has proficiency throwing into coverage. Whenever his receivers or tight end couldn’t completely separate from the defense, Wood threaded the needle into tight spaces in a way that kept his teammates’ running momentum going, often resulting in additional yardage after the reception.

Dual-threat capabilities

Wood’s skill as a passer doesn’t end in the pocket, however. He’s efficient when scrambling out of the pocket and throwing on the run while maintaining sufficient power.

Wood isn’t just a pocket passer, however. He’s equally as strong throwing on the run when the pocket collapses.He keeps a cool head, calmly surveys and makes the best of imperfect situations. pic.twitter.com/0W6vxh9rWd— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

He’s slippery when maneuvering out of the pocket and can evade tackles to keep the play alive. On play-action, Wood is patient in getting himself and his intended receiver free before making the throw, something he can do while running on both sides of the field.

Wood’s pocket awareness allows him to get out of danger before he’s even touched, lending to his crisp uncontested throws.

When he doesn’t have an option downfield and has to run it himself, he’s quick and agile on his feet. He has a 4.67 second 40-yard dash time, and with a background as a track and field athlete, Wood is willing to make deeper runs when he feels he can gain a few extra yards after initial contact rather than opting to slide.

When there aren’t any open receivers, however, Wood isn’t shy about making a break for it himself.He has the long speed to make deep runs down the field into open space. Wood’s grit affords him a few more yards in exchange for taking a shot. pic.twitter.com/WOoM9yYUZH— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

His ability to create additional options for himself when standard pocket progression falls short will make him a threatening quarterback for collegiate defenses to work against.

Mental strength, poise

Wood is known for his calm demeanor and not folding under pressure, which is a big reason why he’s such a decorated high school athlete.

That winning culture Wood’s been surrounded by since his freshman year has fostered confidence in himself to make the big plays when they matter. It's evident in his game by the way he doesn’t back down on an opposing blitz.

When the pocket collapses, Wood doesn’t panic. He scans the field, goes through his progressions and typically makes a well-thought out pass or move to the outside before the rushers can get to him.

Wood is a difficult quarterback to sack due to his pocket awareness and ability to get a step ahead on shedding tackles.He recognizes dangerous situations a beat before they occur and steps up in the pocket for an extra split second to throw. pic.twitter.com/vkzWcdLXaX— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 17, 2026

If he’s forced out of the pocket, Wood always has his eyes looking upfield and more often than not will locate an open target in the nick of time.

All Wood knows is victory, which is clear considering his back-to-back state championship victories as the starter. Arguably one of his most impressive performances came in the 2025 MIAA Division I Super Bowl, where he went 11-16 for 345 yards and five touchdowns.

Wood’s calmness can prove to be his greatest strength as he makes the jump from high school to college, so long as he remains coachable and makes the adjustments he needs to perform well in a Power Four conference like the Big Ten.

title: No. 8 Penn State falls to No. 1 Princeton in NCAA quarterfinal
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
NEWARK, Del. — On Sunday, No. 8 Penn State and No. 1 Princeton met for the second time in the 2026 season, but the rematch at Delaware Stadium was on a much larger stage in the NCAA quarterfinals.

Despite the earlier meeting between the budding rivals ending in Penn State’s favor, the Tigers came out on top with an 14-10 victory to send them through to the national semifinals. The Nittany Lions ended their turbulent season with a 10-6 overall record and Big Ten Tournament championship.

“Congratulations to Coach Madalon, his staff, and his team, they played terrific today, and just a shout out to Penn State’s fans,” coach Jeff Tambroni said. “Just really appreciative of the way they showed up today, and Delaware did a great job of hosting. I’m sorry it didn’t work out for Penn State today, but with that the whole team played really hard and gave great effort.”

First half

While Penn State got off on the right foot, the difference was that the Tigers’ shots made it on goal while the Nittany Lions often missed the net. Hawkins was forced to make the majority of the stops himself, finishing the game with a career-high 17 saves, nine of which came in the first half.

By comparison, Ryan Croddick made eight saves in the victory.

“Penn State (had) such an incredibly disciplined offense,” Princeton coach Matt Madalon said. “I don't think they settled for a shot. With Aquino off the field, they're not taking a shot outside of eight yards, so it's not a game where the goalie is going to get a ton of rhythm.”

The freshman goaltender quickly made his first stop of the game on Colin Burns. On the ensuing Penn State possession, Kyle Lehman gave the ball to Alex Ross in transition and the No. 8 overall pick in the PLL draft found the back of the cage to open the scoring.

Lehman continued to work on and off the ball, spinning the ball around the box and filling an open spot to shoot sidearmed past goaltender Ryan Croddick.

The Tigers proceeded to generate three consecutive golden opportunities to put themselves on the board. However, all three shots struck the iron — both posts and the crossbar — on the same possession.

Jake Vana broke the curse on the next shot, beating his defender 1-on-1 and threading a shot in the bottom right corner under Hawkins’ stick.

Hawkins made good on his next two chances with a high stick save on Nate Kabiri, later making a series of chest saves to keep Penn State in the lead by a goal at the end of the first quarter.

Chase Robertson drove down the middle of the box, splitting defenders and struck the back of the net to regain the two-goal lead with 13:10 remaining in the second quarter. Tucker Wade responded with a shot through traffic to bring the Tigers back within one.

Jackson Green picked off a Penn State pass in the defensive end and brought it up the field in transition. Chad Palumbo finished the job on the doorstep to tie the game, 3-3. John Jude Considine answered with a solo effort through four different Princeton defenders to dunk the ball just past Croddick, taking back the lead with 9:23 remaining in the half.

The game was tied once more when Wade stepped down into a shot that evaded Hawkins. After a media timeout, Burns jumped to snag a high pass from Palumbo and shot five-hole on Hawkins to take, giving Princeton its first lead of the game.

Parker Reynolds got his hands free by spinning away from a double team, Patrick Keenan’s slide coming flatfooted, and laced a shot past Hawkins to take a 6-4 lead.

Robertson and Andrew Beard surged with two-straight tallies late in the second quarter to tie the game 6-6 going into halftime.

Second half

The start of the second half closely mirrored the first. Hawkins immediately made a sprawling save on Nate Kabiri from his knees to start the third quarter strong. Then, Lehman rolled back off a check and fired a shot past Croddick to take a 7-6 lead.

“It was still tough, Princeton’s a really good team and they have a lot of good shooters,” Hawkins said. “But ultimately the defense was in front of me all day, giving me the shots I wanted to see. So they were the reason I was successful today.”

With 11:42 left in the quarter, Mark Watters caught Iannatuono's pass from X and slipped a low shot past Croddick short-side. Then, Porter Malkiel fell outside of the crease on a shot that beat Hawkins for the answer.

Wade secured a hat trick on the day to tie up the game, 8-8, with a bouncing shot under Hawkins’ stick with 9:13 remaining.

After a roughly seven-minute scoring drought, Palumbo scored his second goal of the game to take a 9-8 lead heading into a media timeout.

Liam Matthews tied the game, 9-9, with 45.1 seconds left in the third quarter. He was tripped and slashed on the play, the latter resulting in a minute-long man-up, and fell into a slow bouncing shot that Croddick didn’t see.

Robertson scored 58 seconds into the fourth quarter on a step-down shot that bounced past Croddick.

From there until the end, it was all Princeton.

Green stole yet another pass out of thin air and sparked a transition play to get the ball in Quinn Krammer’s stick for a game-tying goal. Princeton took the lead when John Dunphey let loose an uncontested shot after Palumbo caught a high shot behind the net for the pass, catching the Nittany Lion defense off guard.

Andrew McMeekin scored off the subsequent faceoff, blazing down the middle to give the Tigers a 12-10 lead with just over six minutes remaining in the game. Wade scored the fourth unanswered goal a minute later, forcing Tambroni to call a timeout.

The Tigers iced the game with Cooper Mueller’s goal, evading Penn State’s 10-man ride while Croddick came up big in the end.

“It hurts a lot to see my teammates, my brothers cry, especially our seniors and our captains,” Robertson said. “They helped lead all the way down to our freshmen. I'm so thankful to have every single one of them, and it hurts to have our season go out this way.”

Injury report

Patrick Carragher was listed out with an undisclosed injury that occurred in the Big Ten semifinals against Maryland. Keenan was injured in the third quarter and was out for the rest of the game.

title: Penn State track and field's Ajani Dwyer breaks school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another race, another school record by star sophomore Ajani Dwyer.

Dwyer broke his previous school record in the men's 100-meter in the preliminary round of the Big Ten Championships, running 10.04 seconds.

The Sewell, New Jersey, native set his previous record at the Florida Relays earlier this season and ran an even-faster 9.96 seconds at the Knights Invite, but was ruled ineligible due to wind.

Dwyer has the school record in the 60-meters and 100m, as well as the No. 4 outdoor mark in the 200-meters and No. 2 indoor mark.

title: Penn State track and field competes at Big Ten Championships
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After three months of competition, Penn State suited up for the postseason this weekend.

The Nittany Lions competed at the 2026 Outdoor Big Ten Track and Field Championship from Friday through Sunday, earning 17 podium finishes, an eighth-place finish for the men’s team and an 11th-place finish for the women’s team.

Here’s a look at how the blue and white fared this weekend.

Friday

A trio of Nittany Lions took the field for the women’s hammer throw to kick off the event on Friday.

Senior Alex Pancoast led the way for Penn State, throwing 56.16 meters for 13th place. While Gabi Deglau threw a season best 55.32 meters, she finished just behind in 15th place.

Freshman Avery Miller competed in the first four events of the women’s heptathlon in her first-ever collegiate meet.

Miller put herself near the back of the pack following the 100-meter hurdles, where she ran 14.79 seconds. However, she continued to improve with a ninth-place finish in the high jump for 1.59 meters, a fourth-place finish in shot put for 11.71 meters and a fifth-place finish in the 200-meters with a time of 24.75 seconds. Heading into Saturday, she was in eighth place with 3,146 points.

While graduate student Collin Burkhart didn’t earn a podium finish in his final Big Ten Championship, he improved from last year’s performance, throwing 65.68 meters for seventh place.

In the women’s javelin throw, senior Audrey Friedman recorded her second-best mark of the season, finishing 14th with a throw of 47.20 meters.

In the first individual track event of the competition, senior Matt DeMatteo tied his personal best in the men’s 400-meter hurdles, running 52.17 seconds for 13th overall.

Senior Marie-Eloise Leclair booked her first finals appearance of the weekend in the women’s 200m, running 22.86 seconds wind-aided for seventh overall. She’ll compete in the finals at 3:26 p.m. on Sunday.

The biggest moment of the day for the Nittany Lions came in the men’s javelin throw, where freshman Nolan Carey propelled himself to No. 2 in school history with a seven-meter personal best. Carey threw 74.72 meters to finish fourth overall.

In the men’s 1,500-meters, junior Nick Sloff finished second in his heat and second overall, running 3:43.49 to qualify for Sunday’s finals.

Senior Claire Daniels wrapped up the action on the track Friday night, running 35:34.63 in the women’s 10,000-meters to finish 13th overall.

Saturday

Miller continued her first-ever collegiate heptathlon on Saturday morning, competing in the long jump. The Cumberland, Maryland, native jumped 5.23 meters for ninth place, dropping to ninth overall. However, she came back in the javelin throw with a mark of 38.90 meters for fourth place, moving to sixth overall with 4,415 points.

In the women’s shot put, freshman Skye-Petrie Cameron finished 10th overall with a throw of 16.22 meters. Sophomore Sasha Garnett finished just behind with 15.80 meters for 15th place.

In the women’s high jump, sophomore Hannah Riolo earned the first points for the women’s squad. Riolo tied her personal best of 1.80 meters, finishing in sixth place.

After clinching the 200m finals on Friday, Leclair earned her second finals berth in the 100m, running 11.39 seconds for fifth overall.

In the men’s 100m prelims, sophomore Ajani Dwyer broke the school record with a time of 10.04 seconds, also earning a finals berth as he won the preliminary race.

Miller capped off her first collegiate heptathlon with a first-place finish in the 800-meters, running 2:14.35 to finish with 5,317 points and fifth place. Miller recorded the eighth-best heptathlon in school history.

Penn State continued its success in the 800m as senior Hayley Kitching earned a season-best 2:01.61 to finish second overall and book a spot in the finals.

Graduate student Allison Johnson set the No. 5 mark in school history to finish ninth and earn the final spot in the finals, running 2:03.35, while freshman Boh Ritchie missed out on finals with a time of 2:05.74, finishing 15th overall.

The men’s 800m saw three qualifiers for the Nittany Lions, as graduate student Allon Clay took the top spot with a meet record and No. 3 mark in school history of 1:45.11. Junior Andrew Regnier set the No. 8 mark in school history in 1:46.10 for third place, while Niko Schultz ran 1:46.42 for sixth place.

Penn State wrapped up Saturday’s action in the 3,000-meter steeplechase, where senior Allison Dougherty finished seventh overall with a season-best 10:16.61. In the men’s race, senior Ryan Watson finished fourth in 8:54.19, while Logan Strawser finished eighth in 8:58.77, earning a combined six points.

Saturday

In just his freshman year, Trip Campbell burst onto the scene for the men’s high jump, tying for third place with a mark of 2.12 meters. Robert Allen also finished top-10, jumping 2.04 meters for ninth place.

In the men’s pole vault, senior Tristan McGarrah finished 12th overall with a mark of 5.07 meters.

After finishing second in the 1,500m prelims on Friday, Sloth came back and finished fifth overall in the finals, running a time of 4:02.42 for four points.

In the men’s shot put, graduate student Dylan Lambrecht led the way for Penn State with a top mark of 18.60 meters, finishing in ninth place. Freshman Bryce Thompson finished in 11th place with 18.26 meters, while senior Ryan Henry finished 13th with 18.00 meters.

Leclair took the track for her first of two finals appearances on Sunday, running 11.12 seconds for fifth place in the 100m. On the men’s side, Dwyer also finished fifth, running 9.93 seconds.

Penn State’s first Big Ten champion came in the women’s 800m, as Kitching claimed her second Big Ten outdoor title and fourth overall Big Ten title. The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native ran 2:01.77, followed by Johnson in ninth place in 2:09.90.

The success carried over into the men’s 800m finals, as Clay earned his second straight outdoor 800m title.

Clay ran a mark of 1:46.58 to take the top spot, followed by Schultz’ 1:47.70 for third place and Regnier’s 1:48.72 for eighth place.

Leclair finished her first and only Big Ten championship in the 200m finals, where she finished seventh overall in 22.80 seconds, earning another two points for the Nittany Lions.

The Nittany Lions wrapped up the 2026 Outdoor Big Ten Track and Field Championships in the 4x400m relay.

The women’s team went first, finishing 11th in 3:44.81 with Alexandria North, Abasiono Akpan, Ritchie and Kitching, while the men’s team finished sixth in 3:09.73 with Carter Fitzgerald, Matt DeMatteo, Schultz and Jake Palermo.

Up next

Penn State will compete in the NCAA East First Round, kicking off on Wednesday, May 27 in Lexington, Kentucky. The top 48 athletes in the region for each event will compete for a spot in the NCAA Championships.

title: Former Penn State student charged for unauthorized access of UPPS computer systems, bail set at $2M
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State student has been charged with unlawful use of a computer and computer trespass to alter data, according to a criminal complaint filed on Thursday.

According to an affidavit of probable cause, Zuoyu Wu, a former University Police and Public Safety (UPPS) student auxiliary officer, was reported to be involved in suspicious activity involving public safety communications and radio programming. These reports caused UPPS to launch a criminal investigation into Wu in July 2025.

The affidavit said Wu had expressed interest in UPPS’s Motorola radio systems and requested to be allowed to assist in programming these radios, which the affidavit said he was observed frequently doing, due to having been employed by Motorola.

Wu also claimed to have obtained a “Motorola engineer” certification. The affidavit said no documents have been produced to confirm Wu’s claims to having this certification.

Wu was witnessed to have been accessing computer programs within UPPS’s dispatch officer, which were said to contain personal identifiable information, vehicle registration data and criminal histories, according to the affidavit. Access to these programs is granted only to authorized personnel and not auxiliary officers.

Wu’s preliminary hearing is scheduled for May 20 at 8:30 a.m. He is currently incarcerated at Centre County Correctional Facility and District Judge Steven F. Lachamn set bail at $2 million.
