title: Former Penn State WR Kyron Hudson signs with Chicago Bears
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After an invite to rookie minicamp, Kyron Hudson has earned himself a contract.

Hudson was signed by the Chicago Bears, the team announced Monday, giving the wide receiver a chance to live out his NFL dreams.

During his career at USC, Hudson was one of the most reliable receivers in the country with a 2.7% career drop rate through three seasons. In his junior year, Hudson caught every pass thrown at him.

Things didn’t work out as he’d hoped when he transferred to Penn State for his final season of college football, seeing a near 50% decline in yardage from 2024 to 2025.

While Hudson was never the No. 1 receiver at USC, that same mantra held true at Penn State, finishing as the third-best receiver in receptions, yards and touchdowns.

Despite the disappointing season, the California native embraced the opportunity to play at Penn State, which has now paid off.

“At the end of the day, whatever team I land on is going to be the same thing,” Hudson said at Penn State Pro Day. “I’m gonna get there, I may not know anybody. But I’m gonna go over there, I’m gonna be myself and just be a leader.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball hires Susan Robinson Frutchl as senior director of advancement and strategic relations
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
A Lady Lion legend is coming home.

Former Penn State women's basketball star and athletics gifts officer Susan Robinson Fruchtl was announced as the senior director of advancement and strategic relations for the Lady Lions on Monday.

Welcome back to where it all started, @Susan21Robinson! 🦁#WeAre pic.twitter.com/e2D97UJYtD— Penn State Women's Basketball (@PennStateWBB) May 11, 2026

"I am ecstatic to have Susan rejoin the Lady Lion program," coach Tanisha Wright said in a press release. "Being able to work alongside Susan will be monumental as we reestablish Penn state women’s basketball."

Robinson Fruchtl graduated from Penn State in 1992 with 2,253 points, which was the most in Lady Lion history at the time. She now sits third overall and is the school's only Wade Trophy recipient, also having earned numerous All-American honors and Atlantic 10 honors.

The former Lady Lion spent several years playing professionally in Japan, also serving as an assistant coach for Penn State in 1993.

After receiving her master's degree, Robinson Fruchtl coached North Allegheny High School and Beaver Area High School, before returning to Penn State in 2004, where she helped coach Wright.

Robinson Fruchtl earned her first head coaching job in 2007, spending five years at Saint Francis before spending four years at Providence.

In 2016, Robinson Fruchtl returned to Saint Francis as the director of athletics, before becoming director of development and advancement at Saint Joseph's Catholic Academy and eventually the major gifts officer for Penn State Athletics in 2021.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Historic games, award-winning athletes | Penn State Athletics year in review
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Well, it's been another eventful year in Happy Valley. From the diamond to the ice. While not every sports endured great results, there was historical territory reached throughout the year. With that being said, here's my recap of this year in athletics, and awarding this season’s top moments.

Team of the Year – Women’s Hockey

After three years of not being able to advance past the first round of the NCAA tournament, the Nittany Lions had a historic season and reached their first-ever Frozen Four.

Penn State’s season featured several historic moments, from playing in the first Division 1 hockey game inside Beaver Stadium to earning its highest ranking in program history at No. 3 in the nation. Head coach Jeff Kampersal led his team to a 33-6 season, defeating Mercyhurst for its fourth-straight AHA Championship.

Despite ultimately losing to Wisconsin in overtime of the national semifinal, the Nittany Lions used their season to build the program to its highest level and earn awards along the way.

Notably, Tessa Janecke won her third-straight AHA Player of the Year award and was a finalist for the Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award. Kampersal earned the CCM/AHCA Women’s University Division Coach of the Year award, becoming the first coach to win this award at Penn State.

Also nominated – Wrestling, Men’s Lacrosse

Coach of the Year – Jeff Kampersal, women’s hockey

Since taking the head coaching role for Penn State women’s hockey in 2017, Kampersal took a nonexistent program and turned it into one of the winningest teams on campus today.

Kampersal has led his team to four-straight conference championships and NCAA tournament appearances. In his most recent season, Kampersal reached 500 total wins as a head coach, making him only the fifth women’s hockey coach to reach the milestone.

Although not finishing his most successful season on campus with a national championship, Kampersal’s years of developing a dominant team came to fruition through its first Frozen Four appearance, capping a historic year for the program.

Also nominated – Clarisa Crowell; softball, Cael Sanderson; Wrestling

Moment of the Year – Penn State women’s hockey first Frozen Four

For the first time in program history, the Nittany Lions won an NCAA Women’s Ice Hockey Championship game when they beat UConn in the regional final.

Coming off its all-time winningest season, Penn State carried momentum through its fourth-straight AHA Championship into a deep tournament run. It seemed destined to be, as the 2026 Women’s Frozen Four took place in Happy Valley.

The Nittany Lions were energized from the noise of their fans, advancing as far as they could before being eliminated by Wisconsin in the semifinal.

Game of the Year – Wisconsin in the Frozen Four

A record breaking 5,176 fans filled Pegula Ice Arena in hopes to witness one of the biggest wins in Penn State women’s hockey history. The Nittany Lions faced Wisconsin in the semifinal round of the Frozen Four after defeating UConn 3-0 in the Regional Final.

After Tessa Janecke blew the roof off the arena with the first goal of the game, both teams exchanged scores ultimately leading to a 3-2 Badgers lead with less than 10 minutes left in the game. That was until Janecke received a breakout pass from Nicole Hall that sparked a quick breakaway.

The captain went from her forehand to her backhand and sent the home crowd into a frenzy. Although ultimately losing to Wisconsin in overtime, the game went down as one of Penn State’s most memorable games, and will leave a lasting impact on the players and fans.

Freshman of the Year – Gavin McKenna

After announcing his commitment to Penn State on July 8, Gavin McKenna became one of the most hyped athletes to arrive in Happy Valley.

The 17-year-old phenom had his name on the scoresheet in his first game as a Nittany Lion with a pair of assists, but it wasn’t until the following day where he earned his first collegiate goal that proved to be the game-winner against Arizona State.

McKenna only became more famous at Penn State as the season continued, eventually leading the team with 51 points as the season came to an end. McKenna earned several awards and honors such as the Big Ten Freshman of the Year, a Hobey Baker Award Finalist and Big Ten Scoring Champion.

Also nominated – Rocquette Allen, women’s lacrosse; Jack Iannantuono, men’s lacrosse

Play of the Year – Gavin McKenna goal at Beaver Stadium

It could be hard to make over 74,000 people lose their minds at once, but a game-tying Gavin McKenna goal in the second period did just that. Trailing 2-1 late in the second period to Michigan State, a historic crowd was waiting in angst for a reason to yell.

McKenna received a pass in the slot from Mac Gadowsky and made no mistake with it, firing a quick wrist shot that snuck under the Spartan netminder’s blocker and sent the stadium into an uproar.

The Nittany Lions remained neck-and-neck with Michigan State, ultimately falling in overtime. However, the McKenna goal will be remembered for quite some time through a picture of his celebration in front of the record crowd.

Hang it in the Louvre!! 🏒🖼️ #WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/gyjoAzmFsL— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) January 31, 2026

Also nominated – Tessa Janecke Frozen Four Goal

Upset of the Year – Basketball: Penn State 71, Iowa 69

The Nittany Lions were -9.5 point underdogs as they welcomed a 20-8 Iowa team to the Bryce Jordan Center for a late-season conference game.

At this point, Penn State’s season was all but finished, as they began the day 12-17. There was no chance of reaching a .500 season, and a quick exit in the Big Ten tournament seemed imminent.

However, the Nittany Lions were able to earn their last win of the season in upset fashion with a 71-69 victory over the Hawkeyes. Josh Reed led the charge with 25 points and knocked down 3-of-4 from beyond the arc.

Best Transfer Portal Additions – Michael Anderson, baseball; Kennedy Martin, volleyball

Coach Mike Gambino landed one of the best transfer portal additions in the Big Ten on the season. Michael Anderson has been dominant in his first season for the Nittany Lions.

The Arkansas transfer brought his SEC power to the Big Ten as he currently leads the conference with a .779 slugging percentage and 19 home runs. Anderson has been a noticeable addition to the lineup throughout the season.

Kennedy Martin proved to be one of the best transfer portal additions on the season through a record-setting season with Penn State. The Florida transfer broke the record for points scored in a single season with 728, and continued to shine throughout the NCAA tournament by remaining a key offensive leader.

The Fort Mill, South Carolina, native earned a finalist spot for the AVCA National Player of the Year after a dominant first season as a Nittany Lion.

Most Disappointing Team – Football

Penn State entered the 2025-26 with the highest expectations it’s had in some time. After falling just short to Notre Dame in the semifinals of the college football playoffs, the players were expected to return the following year with revenge on their minds.

However, after starting the season 3-0, the Nittany Lions lost their following six games leading to one of the biggest flops in program history.

The losses seemed never-ending as Drew Allar was sidelined for the remainder of the season due to an ankle injury against Northwestern. Following the same game, James Franklin was fired, putting Terry Smith in the head coach position.

A disastrous and completely unexpected season ended in a 7-6 record, with the Nittany Lions going 3-6 in Big Ten play. After being a few plays away from the championship the season before, Penn State missed the playoffs entirely.

Also nominated – Basketball

Comeback Player of the Year – Bridget Nemeth, softball

Following an injury that kept her out for her sophomore season, Bridget Nemeth returned to Penn State’s pitching rotation in dominant fashion.

The Glendora, California, native heads into the playoffs with a 5.01 ERA through 17 starts. Nemeth earned 57 strikeouts throughout her returning year, the third most on the team.

Although not anywhere close to her freshman year total at 202, the junior proved that her season-ending injury the season before didn’t deter her in her comeback year.

title: Penn State men's lacrosse earns Inside Lacrosse postseason honors
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
A stellar season just got even better.

The blue and white laid claim to a four-way tie for the Big Ten regular season title, but continued with an emphatic postseason. Penn State secured the conference title with wins over Maryland and John Hopkins, posting a score differential of 24-14.

After that strong performance in the Big Ten postseason, a squad of Nittany Lions earned recognition for their play throughout the year. As momentum rides high, three Penn State players earned Inside Lacrosse All-American honors.

More postseason honors for this crowd. pic.twitter.com/YHYTG3ucPU— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) May 10, 2026

The accolades earned by the blue and white are headlined by defenseman Alex Ross, who earned a national first-team nod. The senior was an honorable mention by Inside Lacrosse last season, but after a year with 21 caused turnovers, he finally broke through to the first team.

Joining Ross with national honors is short stick defensive midfielder Jon King, who was selected as a second-team All-American. Also, in his senior year, this is King's first time earning All-American honors after logging 17 caused turnovers, a figure that trailed only Ross.

Rounding out the group of award winners is faceoff specialist Reid Gillis, who earned recognition as an honorable mention. Gillis was the primary option in the circle, with a team high .632 winning percentage.

All three Nittany Lions who earned end-of-year accolades were also featured as Inside Lacrosse's midseason All-Americans.

After defeating Army 10-6 in the first round of the NCAA Tournament, Penn State will continue its quest for glory on Sunday, May 17 in the national quarterfinal hosted by the University of Delaware.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: ‘One day at a time’ | Former Penn State QB Drew Allar set on growth at Steelers rookie minicamp
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
At the Pittsburgh Steelers rookie minicamp on Saturday, Drew Allar found himself going back to the basics.

His footwork, a notable weakness that continued to reappear during Allar’s time at Penn State, was purposefully exaggerated. The new coaching regime under Mike McCarthy is focused on breaking down and rebuilding the foundations of Allar’s game, so it wasn’t meant to look complete — it was meant to form good habits and muscle memory.

Allar was the lone quarterback in attendance at the camp, spending his time wisely by asking a lot of questions to soak up as much information as possible, whether that’s on the system or areas where he needs to improve most.

“I was able to ask a ton of questions and I’ll have a great group of quarterbacks here on Monday, so it’ll be even better to learn from the guys that have been in the system for a handful of weeks more than me,” Allar said at minicamp. “I’m really looking forward to it.”

Being the only quarterback on the field, Allar got to experience direct one-on-one coaching from McCarthy. The pair primarily worked on Allar’s footwork and fundamentals while the young signal caller picked up on the specific terminology that the team uses.

“It’s a little bit different than what I’m used to,” Allar said. “I just gotta take it one day at a time and find ways to be more consistent at it and improve on it each day because I might’ve started at one spot but if I can find a way to get better each day, I can accelerate my growth. That’s the main goal, to learn as much as I can from the quarterbacks in the room and the coaches.”

Allar has drawn a few comparisons to Steelers legend Ben Roethlisberger, as both possess similar superior size and unmatched arm strength. For Allar to get to Roethlisberger’s level, however, he first must win the starting job over 2025 sixth-round selection Will Howard.

With Howard already having a year in the NFL under his belt, Allar’s growth is imperative to him potentially fulfilling the role as the next franchise quarterback. But this year, that’s dependent on whether Aaron Rodgers returns to Pittsburgh.

“(Allar) is physically gifted, he’s a gifted thrower of the football, but he’s no different than any other young quarterback that I’ve had the opportunity to work with,” McCarthy said. “Everybody teaches footwork a little differently, everybody has a system of offense and how you tie your quarterback particularly into the pass game. There’s a lot of work there to adjust some fundamentals that we think will help him.”

Rodgers has a scheduled visit with the team, so Allar is likely to be a backup for his rookie season and use the time to continue preparing and potentially learning under an all-time great quarterback. For Allar, refinement is top of the mind.

“I know what I need to work on and how to work on it.” Allar said. “I just have to be intentional with doing the same routine every day and making sure that I’m staying on top of the little things.”

However, Allar is keeping a calm demeanor about the situation. He’s been in this scenario before, albeit not as a professional, as a former 5-star recruit brought in as the quarterback who could bring the Nittany Lions back to the national championship.

While that didn’t pan out, Allar appears to have learned from experiencing that kind of pressure, and more importantly, how to deal with it.

“I don’t think it’s overwhelming at all, it’s a lot of fun,” Allar said. “This is what I’ve worked for basically since high school to have the opportunity to play in the NFL. So I’m definitely not taking it for granted. And with a great organization, so I couldn’t be more happy or excited for myself to be in this position.”

title: No. 8 Penn State men’s lacrosse overpowers No. 11 Army to claim victory in NCAA Tournament first round
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 8 Penn State stayed at Panzer Stadium on Saturday afternoon for the first round of the NCAA Men’s Lacrosse Championship, hosting No. 11 Army for the chance to keep its season alive.

The blue and white overwhelmed the Black Knights and carved the path to a 10-6 victory despite the 20-save performance by Patriot League Goaltender of the Year Sean Byrne.

First half

Penn State came out the gates swinging after Colby Baldwin’s patience on the face-off dot won the Nittany Lions the opening possession. The Nittany Lions dominated the dot, going 16-for-20 as a team, directly contributing to the mountain of offense and sustained pressure throughout the game.

Both Chase Robertson and John Jude Considine had shots on Byrne, but it was Hunter Aquino’s shot on the doorstep that broke the ice less than two minutes into the first quarter.

However, Army’s Grant Masterson knocked Robertson down on the following Penn State possession to cause a turnover. Evan Plunkett followed up by going over the top for a shot past Preston Hawkins to tie the game.

Army didn’t score a goal for the next 20 minutes, as Penn State’s defense continued to thwart the Black Knights’ advancements, causing turnovers and making key plays in transition. In addition, the Nittany Lions appeared more comfortable playing on the slick, wet field as Army’s players often slipped.

Aquino responded with his second goal of the game with 8:25 remaining in the quarter, receiving a no-look pass from Andrew Beard before sending a side-arm shot past Byrne.

After a six-minute scoring drought, Michael Faraone got Penn State back on the board to increase the Nittany Lions’ lead to two. He found a lane on the left side, caught a pass from Jack Iannantuono behind the crease and struck the cage short side.

Penn State had more opportunities to score but were stopped on the doorstep by Byrne who kept the game close.

Beard solved Byrne with a run down the right side before firing a three-quarters shot between the goaltender’s legs to take a three-goal lead.

Finally, Plunkett found Brayden Fountain all alone on the crease who tapped a one-timer past Hawkins to cut the deficit to 4-2. Then — after an offensive-zone turnover by Robertson — Hill Plunkett took the ball himself and bullied his way past Lucca DiBartolomeo to score a bouncing shot.

Beard responded with his second goal of the game with 3:40 remaining in the half, spinning past Masterson before a blistering shot beat Byrne. Luke Walstrum added to the lead with just over a minute left, capping off a series of smooth passes around the box to keep Army guessing.

With eight seconds remaining, Hawkins made a stick save on a low shot to maintain the three-goal advantage.

Second half

Army kicked off the third quarter with a stop on defense and capitalized on its first offensive possession. A pass by Hill Plunkett tipped off Alex Ross’ stick and landed in the crease for Gunnar Fellows to dunk.

However, Penn State answered right back on a failed clearance by the Black Knights. Aquino intercepted Byrne’s pass from the crease and then ripped a side-arm shot into the top left corner of the net.

The next 10 minutes passed with a scoring drought, as Byrne and Hawkins came up big for their teams going into the fourth quarter.

Army put pressure on Hawkins with six minutes remaining in the third quarter and Tyler Clark’s shot appeared to beat the freshman netminder. However, he reached his stick behind him and stopped the ball before it crossed the goal line.

Kyle Lehman broke the trend by himself at the beginning of the final frame, weaving his way past Isaiah Rose with a series of face dodges to cut into the middle for a low shot past Byrne to take a four-goal.

After Hawkins made a save on a high shot, Brendan Leary scored his first goal of the season in transition to make it 9-4. He was pushed in the back by Clark in the process, but the penalty was waived due to the goal.

Faraone added an exclamation point on the afternoon with a behind-the-back shot that fooled Byrne to take a six-goal lead with less than nine minutes to go.

Army at last got over its 28-minute scoring drought as Fountain picked up a loose rebound on the doorstep and beat a flailing Hawkins to cut the lead to 10-5. Then, Tyler Mathews cut Penn State’s lead to four with a bouncing side-arm shot less than a minute later.

Beard ran out much of the clock, giving Army less time to work with on offense. Hawkins made two final saves to win his first-career national tournament game. The freshman finished the game with 12 saves.

Up Next

Penn State will travel to Newark, Delaware, to take on the winner between No. 1 Princeton and Marist in the national quarterfinals on May 17 at Delaware Stadium. The start time is to be determined.

title: Film review | 4-star DL commit Stanley Montgomery possesses elite mobility, frame
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State claimed another blue-chip player on the defensive side of the ball last week with the addition of 2027 4-star Stanley Montgomery, a defensive lineman from Philadelphia.

Montgomery is the second-highest rated Penn State recruit in the class per the 247Sports composite, sitting only behind cornerback Kei’Shjuan Telfair. Montgomery is the Nittany Lions’ highest-ranked defensive tackle commit in over a decade.

Montgomery finished up his junior year at Archbishop Ryan High School with 45 tackles including eight sacks and 19 tackles for loss. He was named first-team All-Catholic for both the defensive and offensive line as a result.

His tape showcases his readiness for college football on a Power Four team, as Montgomery has the build, mobility and raw power to make a splash when he arrives on campus.

Collegiate-caliber frame

The 6-foot-3, 300-pound defensive tackle already possesses the build to fit in at Penn State. However, he should be able to use the time during his senior year of high school and offseason leading up to his freshman year of college to bulk up even further to match that of a starter.

Montgomery also has the wingspan to make up for times when he’s pushed off his line, as he consistently gets a hand on quarterbacks or rushers even when the offensive guard is holding him relatively at bay.

In addition, once he has a hand on the ball carrier, he typically gets them on the ground. His long wingspan allows him to wrap his arms around the opponent to make tackles from the front, behind and flanks, making Montgomery a reliable defender from nearly any angle.

Montgomery has the ability to improvise and make the most out of less-than-perfect opportunities, often using his wingspan to disrupt or make a sneaky tackle. Here's him with a forced fumble: pic.twitter.com/xXVMhNJU3l— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 8, 2026

His size and quickness off the jump often puts immediate pressure on quarterbacks, and that force has resulted in bad throws that translate to incompletions and interceptions.

If the quarterback decides to hold on to the ball or hand it off to the running back, Montgomery’s ability to improvise has led to him forcing fumbles, as he tends to either rip or punch at the ball upon impact.

Explosiveness

It’s not just Montgomery’s build that lends to his talent, but his explosiveness as well. Montgomery is heavy off the snap and gets underneath his opponents’ arms to force them off balance.

In those split seconds, he has the advantage and ability to make a swift cut to either side, depending on if the quarterback stays in the pocket or scrambles to the outside.

His build is collegiate-level despite falling short of Penn State's current starters in weight. However, he's got the raw strength to bully his opponent in 1-on-1 battles. Here's Montgomery stampeding en route to the point of handoff on a run play: pic.twitter.com/oZOU8GjfxY— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 8, 2026

Montgomery utilizes raw upper-body strength to bully his opponents in the backfield, often pushing his matchup all the way to the handoff point, stopping runs before they break the line of scrimmage.

Additionally, his explosiveness allows Montgomery to keep up with sudden maneuvers or make recoveries when he needs to fill open gaps elsewhere on the line. His first step is often powerful enough to get him into position to make a tackle.

Montgomery knows when he has to switch and is able to disengage from the offensive line with momentum, launching himself towards any gap that needs to be filled or make a late tackle. pic.twitter.com/chUden91SS— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 8, 2026

Montgomery’s stronger against offenses featuring a quarterback that primarily stays in the pocket instead of moving to the outside, as there are more variables at play than when Montgomery can simply clog the middle.

Mobility

Despite his large build, Montgomery possesses the agility and mobility to make quick decisions in the heat of the moment. After slamming into his matchup, he often follows up with a quick swim move over the top of his opponent’s head, forcing the block to his side so he can slip free into the back field.

It’s a maneuver that’s translated to his sacks and tackles for loss, as it causes the entire offensive line to collapse in hopes of containing him.

He often gets under his opponents to break their guard before going over the top with his arm in a swimming motion, slipping past his match up on the blitz.Here's a few examples of him performing the move: pic.twitter.com/5IXPv5FHS0— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 8, 2026

Should the running back break free past the line of scrimmage, Montgomery opts to perform a spin maneuver to turn and carry momentum towards the streaking rusher, stopping ball carriers before they get further than a handful of yards.

He's quick on his feet and with his eyes, evidenced by how he's able to execute spin moves and jukes to lose the offensive line before immediately locating the ball carrier. pic.twitter.com/LsspSHB1yd— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 8, 2026

Montgomery makes that possible with his mental quickness with shifting between targets and assessing situations — his body is able to make those spur-of-the-moment plays come to fruition. That mental fortitude is a strength on-par with physical advantages at the collegiate level.

title: Penn State baseball bounces back, takes down Washington in doubleheader finale
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
After an unsuccessful comeback bid in Game 1 of Friday’s doubleheader with Washington, Penn State took to the diamond just 45 minutes later for round two.

The Nittany Lions were able to right the ship in the second game, taking the Huskies down 6-4.

The top of the first inning saw Washington pick up where it left off in the series opener, as a walk and two straight singles loaded the bases before the blue and white could record an out.

A sacrifice fly from Colton Bower brought in the runner from third to give the Huskies an early 1-0 advantage, but the Penn State defense was able to record two more outs to stop the damage there.

Leadoff hitter Jayden Davis ripped a double to center field on the second pitch he faced, and would soon be driven home when Kevin Karstetter singled up the middle. He then stole second base before a flyout ended the inning.

Washington got another baserunner in the top of the second inning after a double from Casen Taggart, but the Huskies couldn’t capitalize and send him home. Following that, the blue and white were retired in order to end the second inning.

To open the third frame, Jackson Hotchkiss drew a walk for Washington, but a groundout followed by two strikeouts stranded him as well.

After a groundout by Preston Yaucher, the Penn State offense began to hum in the bottom of the third. A walk by Davis was followed by a single from Anderson, and a wild pitch advanced them both.

Up to bat with two runners threatening, Bryce Molinaro worked the count full and delivered a single that plated both runners, giving Penn State a 3-1 advantage.

Following the offensive output, the Nittany Lion defense stepped up as well, notching a 1-2-3 inning to bring the home bats back to the plate.

Penn State kept the momentum rolling in the bottom of the fourth with a leadoff home run from Jesse Jaconski. On the next at-bat, Maddox McDonald singled, stole second and advanced to third on a groundout. A flyout from Yaucher brought him in to make the score 5-1 in favor of the blue and white.

After a single in the inning’s second at-bat, Hotchkiss sent one out of the park to cut the Nittany Lions’ lead to two in the top of the fifth. Looking to respond, Molinaro drew a walk, but three consecutive Nittany Lions were retired to end the inning.

Washington got another run back in the next inning, when a sacrifice fly from Braeden Terry brought Taggart in to lessen the Penn State lead to just one run.

Jaconski singled to open the bottom of the sixth inning, with Joey DeMucci following behind with the same result. Yet another single, this one from Davis, advanced DeMucci and brought Jaconski home for what would ultimately be the final run scored in the contest.

The remainder of the matchup featured strong defense, as the teams combined for just three hits in the final three innings. In the top of the ninth inning, Mason Horwat struck out the first two batters he faced, before a groundout from Bower sealed the 6-4 victory for Penn State.

Up Next

Penn State will close the series against Washington with a rubber match on Saturday or Sunday, at a time still to be determined.

title: Penn State men's lacrosse set for NCAA Tournament opener against Army
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
On the heels of a 16-8 dismantling of Johns Hopkins in the Big Ten Tournament championship, the Nittany Lions are ready to hunt for a national championship.

There are a lot of words that could be used to describe Penn State’s regular season, but perhaps the most fitting would be “turbulent.”

There were highs, like a win over current No. 1-seed Princeton, as well as victories against Big Ten foes Maryland and Ohio State, but unexplainable losses to the likes of Villanova and Navy defined the campaign just as much as the triumphs.

Arguably, the lowest moment for the blue and white came on Senior Day, when it fell to a then 5-7 Michigan team on home turf. Since then, however, Penn State hasn’t lost a game, and it’s looked better than ever.

Along with its conference championship trophy, Penn State received an automatic bid into the NCAA Tournament, which it’s set to open on Saturday with a first-round clash against Army.

The Black Knights hail from the Patriot League and earned the trip to the national tournament after claiming the championship.

Army came out blazing in the nonconference slate, jumping out to an unblemished 5-0 start on the year. The stretch featured resume-building wins over Rutgers, Yale and Michigan that began to cement its status as one of the best non-power conference teams in the nation.

The team’s three losses came in consecutive weeks, when the Black Knights dropped close ones to Patriot League foes Lafayette and Boston University before a one-goal loss to a North Carolina squad that entered the NCAA Tournament as the No. 3 overall seed.

Following that loss, Army hasn’t dropped another game to date. It cruised through the conference tournament, beating Lehigh 13-6 before claiming the title with a 14-7 victory over Loyola Maryland.

A pair of brothers lead the Black Knights’ offense, as midfielder Evan Plunkett set the team’s pace with 62 points on the year, followed close behind by his brother Hill Plunkett, who has recorded 53. Hill has recorded 29 goals in comparison to his older brother’s 23, but Evan’s 39 assists on the year rank among the top ten nationally.

The Black Knights are stifling without the ball, ranking fourth nationally in scoring defense and third nationally in man-down defense.

Army has dominated the all-time series with Penn State, leading 29-4 all-time. The Nittany Lions have claimed the last three victories, however, winning in a 2023 NCAA quarterfinal as well as back-to-back years in 1998 and 1999.

Faceoff is set for 2:30 p.m. at Panzer Stadium on Saturday, a venue that should be rocking after the team called for White Out conditions in what will be the blue and white’s final home game of the year, regardless of the outcome.

title: Staff Predictions | How will Penn State men’s lacrosse fare in NCAA Tournament?
author: The Daily Collegian Men's Lacrosse Staff
description:
With a win in the Big Ten championship, Penn State has secured its fourth consecutive berth in the NCAA Tournament.

In the last three years, the Nittany Lions have won four tournament games and made two appearances on championship weekend. They haven’t been able to get past the national semifinal in that stretch, however.

In 2023, Penn State controversially fell to Duke in overtime, when a goal from Garrett Leadmon appeared to have been scored after the attackman’s foot entered the crease. Last season, the Nittany Lions suffered a far less divisive defeat, losing to eventual national champion Cornell 11-9.

This season, the blue and white are slated to face Army in the opening round, hosting the Black Knights at Panzer Stadium on Saturday. Will it be the start of another magical run, or simply a first-round exit after an up-and-down season?

Elliott Inscho: Loss in quarterfinals

Penn State has a pretty tough draw in this bracket.

A matchup with Army in the first round seems like a relatively solid break at first, but the Black Knights have built a strong resume on their way to a Patriot League championship.

Army took down Rutgers, Yale and Michigan in its nonconference slate, while losing 12-11 to a North Carolina team that enters the NCAA Tournament as the No. 3 seed. At 13-3, the squad is tied with Princeton for the second-most wins by any tournament team.

Still, Army is seeded lower than the Nittany Lions for a reason. Two of its losses came to Lafayette and Boston, and Penn State is coming into the tournament with the most momentum it’s had all season.

I think the blue and white will handle business at home against Army, but the quarterfinals are where things would really get challenging for the Nittany Lions. Pending a victory over Marist, a trip to Newark, Delaware, would likely see the blue and white match up with No. 1 Princeton.

The Nittany Lions have already defeated the Tigers this year, but it came in Princeton’s first game of the season. It's only lost one game since then, a 13-11 nail-biter to Cornell that was avenged with a 19-9 dismantling in the Ivy League championship game.

Both teams would enter the matchup hot, but Princeton is far, far better than when the Nittany Lions took them down earlier this year. |

Nate Kabiri leads all Tigers with 68 points on the campaign, and was named a Tewaaraton finalist alongside Syracuse attackman Joey Spallina, North Carolina attackman Owen Duffy, Cornell attackman Willem Firth and Notre Dame defenseman Shawn Lyght, all of whom will also be playing for the national title.

Kabiri scored two goals and recorded an assist against Penn State, but has notched four or more points in his last 12 consecutive games. I think that he, alongside with the rest of Princeton’s high-powered offense, will power the Tigers to a win over Penn State in the quarterfinal.

That would end Penn State’s year at 10-6, just a win short of another championship weekend appearance.

Andrew Mercer: Loss in national championship

Penn State has a gauntlet to run through if it wants to make it over the hump and get into the national championship game.

As one of the nation’s most inconsistent teams, the Nittany Lions are in a weird spot heading into their tournament game against Army. They’ve dropped games to unranked teams such as Villanova, Navy and Michigan but have also taken down some of the best teams in the country, like Cornell, Yale and Princeton.

I reason that Penn State and Princeton are two of the hottest teams in the tournament as of the start, with the foes heading into the bracket as champions of its respective conferences. Both teams are on the same side of the bracket as well, so a potential tournament-deciding game could be played in the quarterfinals when the pair meets.

The Nittany Lions’ victory over Johns Hopkins in the Big Ten championship was convincing enough to keep Penn State in the national championship conversation — if and only if the blue and white play its best.

However, Penn State’s star attackman, Hunter Aquino, injured his leg in the title game. He’s questionable on whether or not he’ll be ready to go for the first tournament game and if the injury is worse than originally thought — and he’s out for the season — the Nittany Lions are in hot water.

That said, Penn State still has a solid attack group of Jack Iannantuono, Kyle Lehman and Liam Matthews. It’s just a matter of whether or not that squad clicks and midfielders like Chase Robertson and Michael Faraone chip in. If so, Penn State remains lethal. If not, coach Jeff Tambroni’s team will be toast.

I believe that Penn State makes it past Princeton in the quarterfinals and takes that momentum into the next round to finally appear in the national championship game. However, with Penn State’s tendency to falter in the second half of games, I have the feeling that the Nittany Lions will come up short in the big game despite putting up an electric first half.

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse eliminated by Stanford in NCAA Tournament
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
In the first season under coach Kayla Treanor, Penn State found itself with a date with Stanford in the first round of the NCAA Tournament.

Despite an early lead by the Cardinal, it was a back-and-forth first half and silent second half by Penn State as Stanford took a 7-5 victory.

Just sixty seconds into action, the Cardinal took advantage of the opening draw and scored quickly from Lindsey Devir, taking an early 1-0 lead.

After both teams had another possession on offense, the Nittany Lions had a good look at scoring off a free position attempt, but fell short from a Payton Wainman shot.

Stanford took the save back down to the other end for another offensive opportunity, scoring again. Aliya Polisky scored her first goal of the game and 45th of the season. A little over a minute later, however, the blue and white cut into the lead as Erika Ho scored her first of the day.

That first score marked the beginning of a big run for the Nittany Lions, who went on to take a 3-2 lead through the second quarter.

Following Ho’s goal, things slowed down for seven minutes, but picked back up with a free position goal by senior Emma Kelly, who scored her 16th goal of the season. Despite a turnover for Penn State after a charge on Kelly, the Nittany Lions’ run continued with a goal from Delaney Radin.

On a 3-0 run, the blue and white continued to take shots on offense, as Ho, Kelly MacKinney and Radin combined for four scoring attempts.

However, Stanford found its first success on offense since early in the first quarter, ending a 17-minute cold stretch with a goal from Jordyn Case. That goal sparked a back-and-forth scoreboard change, as Wainman scored off a free position attempt followed by another Stanford goal, this time by Annabel Frist.

The Nittany Lions took the lead heading into the half as Kelly scored her second goal of the game on a no-look score.

Despite a higher-scoring first half, things were slow to begin the second half. After five minutes of no score for either side, the Cardinal capitalized off a Penn State turnover as Polisky scored her second goal of the day, tying things at 5-5.

Things continued through the third quarter with no scoring from either side, taking the tie game into the final 15 minutes.

Penn State had a chance to take the lead early in the fourth quarter as MacKinney took a no-look shot on goal. While the shot wasn’t ruled a goal, the shot went under review and stood as a no-goal.

Stanford took the no-call momentum on the ensuing play as Devir scored her second goal of the game to take a 6-5 lead. A little over a minute later, the Cardinal scored their second straight goal as Lexi Rodell scored her first goal of the game.

Up by two goals, Stanford continued to keep Penn State’s offense silent. The Cardinal defense held the Nittany Lions without a score for the last 31 minutes and 46 seconds of the game, taking their two-goal lead to victory as Penn State was eliminated in the NCAA Tournament.

title: Penn State baseball falls to Washington in series opener
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
With the regular season winding to a close, Penn State entered their final homestand of the season outside of the Big Ten tournament field.

A three-game series with Washington starting on Friday offered a chance for the Nittany Lions to make a late push for postseason play, or at least end the year on a high note.

In a largely defensive battle that saw several runs scored in the late innings, Penn State fell to the Huskies 5-4 in the first game of Friday’s doubleheader.

A leadoff triple from Braeden Terry gave the Huskies a threatening runner early in the first inning, and the graduate outfielder scored on the next at-bat after a sacrifice fly from Jackson Hotchkiss.

Washington got another knock on a double from Colton Bower, but the blue and white’s defense was able to end the inning surrendering just the single run.

In their first trip to the plate, Penn State was sat down in order to close the opening frame. Washington opened the second inning with a double from Ty Cowan, but three consecutive outs afterwards saw the Husky advantage remain at 1-0.

Two quick outs in the bottom of the second inning brought Avery Smith to the plate, who posted a triple before a pop out stranded him just 90 feet from home.

Bower hit his second double of the game with one away in the top of the third inning, but it didn’t result in any change to the scoreboard after Mic Paul struck out looking.

A strikeout and two flyouts saw Penn State retired in order for the second time in three innings, bringing the contest into the fourth inning with Washington still holding their narrow lead.

Two straight groundouts opened the fourth inning before Casen Taggart singled to right field, ultimately advancing to second base on a wild pitch. A walk to Ethan Swidler put an additional runner on first, but a Terry groundout ended the half-frame.

Bryce Molinaro knocked a double to right field with one away in the bottom of the fourth inning, but the Nittany Lions couldn’t take advantage and the score remained 1-0 in favor of the Huskies.

For the first time in the game, Penn State got a three-up three-down inning in the top of the fifth and retired the Washington batters in order.

With a 0-2 count, Jesse Jaconski hit a deep drive to the outfield, but it couldn't leave the ballpark and was caught for the second out in the bottom of the fifth inning.

A walk by Maddox McDonald kept the frame alive for Penn State, and Preston Yaucher took advantage of the extra opportunity by driving a ball to center field and over the fence to thrust the Nittany Lions into the lead.

Paul led off the inning with a walk before Cowan singled to give the Huskies two runners on with one down in the top of the sixth. As a result, coach Mike Gambino brought Ben DeMell into the game to replace starter Colin Fitzgerald.

DeMell got out of the inning after a Washington popout and strikeout. Penn State held the 2-1 lead heading to the bottom of the sixth.

In the top of the seventh, Hotchkiss doubled to left field on the opening at-bat. A single by the next batter advanced him to third base, before he came home on a groundout from Bower to tie the game at 2-2.

Jaconski singled with two outs in the bottom of the seventh, but an ensuing strikeout by McDonald ended the inning.

After two straight walks in the top of the

eighth inning, a three-run homer from Swidler blew the game open late. The Nittany Lions were able to stop the bleeding, but not before Washington took a 5-2 lead.

With two away in the bottom of the eighth, Michael Anderson singled to keep the inning alive. Molinaro homered on the next at bat to give Penn State hope as they headed to the ninth inning down one run.

The Nittany Lions were able to get a 1-2-3 inning in the top of the ninth. Jaconski notched a double with two away and the game on the line, but he was stranded in scoring position after a flyout from the next Penn State batter.

Up Next

Penn State will take on Washington in the second game of Friday’s doubleheader at approximately 5:50 p.m.

title: Former Penn State players get initial NFL numbers, rookie contracts revealed
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
All of Penn State’s 2026 draftees have had their initial NFL numbers revealed ahead of rookie minicamp.

Baltimore Ravens first-round pick Vega Ioane will wear No. 71, the same number he wore at Penn State. Ioane signed a 4-year, $24.2 million deal with a $14 million signing bonus.

Former Penn State quarterback Drew Allar will wear No. 16 for the Pittsburgh Steelers. Allar's number with the Nittany Lions, No. 15, was unavailable, as it's currently being worn by wide receiver Ben Skowronek.

Dallas Cowboys draftee Drew Shelton will don No. 67, signing a 4-year, $5.575 million contract. Similarly, Green Bay Packers draft pick, Dani Dennis-Sutton inked a 4-year, $5.53 million deal with a $1.15 million signing bonus. Dennis-Sutton is set to wear No. 51.

Nick Singleton will wear No. 32 for the Tennessee Titans, while Kaytron Allen will don No. 31, the reversal of his No. 13 with the Nittany Lions.

Defensive linemen Zane Durant will wear No. 92 for the Buffalo Bills, and Carolina Panthers draft pick Zakee Wheatley will wear No. 38.

Undrafted free agent signee Tyler Duzansky signed with the Las Vegas Raiders, earning a 3-year, 3.11 million deal with $110,000 guaranteed and a $10,000 signing bonus. He is wearing No. 46, one digit lower than his number at Penn State.

Other undrafted free agents include Nick Dawkins and Dom DeLuca, who both signed with the Baltimore Ravens. Dawkins will wear No. 53 and DeLuca will wear No. 42.

Wide receiver Trebor Peña will wear No. 86 for the Jacksonville Jaguars.

title: Penn State announces game time for Homecoming game against Wisconsin
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State knows its first game theme for the 2026 season.

The Nittany Lions revealed that their game against Wisconsin on Saturday, Sept. 26 will be the Homecoming matchup.

The teams will meet for the first time since 2024, when the Badgers hosted Penn State and lost to the Nittany Lions 28-13.

The Homecoming game against the Badgers this year comes two weeks earlier than last season's Homecoming contest against Northwestern on Oct. 11, 2025.
